About MA TH2702
This module is MA TH2702 Stochastic Processes . The module manager and lecturer is Dr Benjamin
Lees, the email address for the module is MATH2702@leeds.ac.uk .
Organisation of MA TH2702
This module lasts for 11 weeks, from 29 September to 12 December.
Notes
The main way I expect you to learn the material for this course is reading these notes and by attending
lectures and workshops. I will set two sections of notes each week, for a total of 22 sections. At the start
of each week I will make an announcement of which sections we will cover and what material (such as
assessments) has been made available.
Reading mathematics is a slow process. Each section roughly corresponds to one lecture. Y ou should
read each sentence of explanation and each line of mathematics carefully , including understanding the
motivation as well as checking the accuracy . If you are regularly doing this with new material in much
less than, say , 45 minutes, you might not be reading carefully enough. When it comes to revising lectures
that you have already studied it is not unusual that you might be faster.
It is possible (but not recommended) to learn the material by only reading the notes and completing
the problem sheets. However, for the vast majority of students, it is foolish to not take advantage of the
explanations provided in lectures and workshops and the opportunity to ask questions.
Y ou are probably reading the web version of the notes. If you want a PDF copy (to read offline or to
print out), then click the PDF button in the top ribbon of the page. (W arning: The PDF might not be
as neat and tidy as the web version.)
These notes are heavily based on a previous version of this module and notes of previous years produced
by Matthew Aldridge, I am very grateful to him for providing such a good resource to work with. Even so,
I have made significant modifications to the notes, so I am very keen to hear about errors mathematical,
typographical or otherwise, as these errors are my fault and my responsibility to correct. Please email
me if think you may have found any .
Problem sheets
There will be 11 problem sheets; Problem Sheet ūĚĎõcovers the material from the two sections from week
ūĚĎõ(Sections 2ūĚĎõ‚ąí1 and2ūĚĎõ ), and will be discussed in your workshop in week ūĚĎõ+1 .
Lectures
Lectures will be twice a week and each will aim to cover one section of course material. Lectures in week
ūĚĎõwill cover sections 2ūĚĎõ‚ąí1 and2ūĚĎõ . I will assume you have completed all the work for the previous week
by the time of the lecture, but I will not assume you have started the work for that week itself.
Additionally , there will be one workshop a week. I am very keen to hear about any particular things
you would like to go through in the workshops; please email me , or your workshop leader with your
suggestions.
W orkshops
There will be 10 workshops, starting in the second week. The main goal of the workshops will be to go
over your answers to the problems sheets in smaller classes. Y ou will have been assigned to one of the
workshop groups, led by Nadhir Ben Rached, El Mehdi Haress, Anna Hayward, Jennifer Sexton, or me.
My recommended approach to problem sheets and workshops is the following:
‚ÄĘ W ork through the problem sheet before the workshop, spending plenty of time on it if you can, and
making multiple efforts at questions you get stuck on. Each problem sheet may take several hours
in total, in more than one block. Collaboration is encouraged when working through the problems,
but write up your work on your own.
7

‚ÄĘ T ake advantage of the smaller group setting of the workshop to ask for help or clarification on
questions you were not able to complete.
‚ÄĘ After the workshop, attempt the questions you were previously stuck on.
‚ÄĘ If you are still unable to complete a question after this second round of attempts, then consult the
solutions.
Inevitably a lot of students will come to the problems class without having made a reasonable (or any)
attempt at the problems. Some of these students may expect that the class teacher should simply write
out the solutions on the board to be copied. I am sure we all know this is not a particularly good way
to learn the material, especially when solutions are provided later, and it is much better for classes to
work on a couple of questions and really understand what is happening than to rapidly dash through
everything. Please do not expect or try to demand that class teachers do this and do try to come to
class with some questions or (hopefully) answers, this has the added benefit that it makes the class much
nicer to teach and participate in.
Assessments
There will be two pieces of assessed coursework, making up a total of 15% of your mark for the module.
Assessments 1 will cover the first ~1/2 -2/3 of the course and involve writing up answers to a few problems,
in a similar style to the problem sheets, and is worth 10%. Assessment 2 will involve writing a (very)
short essay that chooses a particular topic in the course and how it fits into the wider course material.
Last year, some students wanted fewer assessments, and there is a general push to reduce the number of
assessments across modules, this is in response to that feedback. Apologies to the (small number?) of
students who wanted more assessed work.
Copying, plagiarism and other types of cheating are not allowed and will be dealt with in accordance
with University procedures. Note that plagiarism means trying to pass off work that you have not done
yourself as your own, this includes work done by other humans or by AI software. LLMs often write
mathematics in an obvious and quite poor way . It is possible with effort to produce something that has
a chance to fool me (or at least make it very diÔ¨Äicult to prove you did not write it) but consider that this
will usually require a decent understanding of the material itself, in which case why did you bother? Also
if you do not understand the material how are you sure that there is not some obvious sign that you have
not written it? Last year the correlation between ‚Äúmost likely used an LLM to cheat on assignments‚ÄĚ
and ‚Äúdid relatively poorly on the exam‚ÄĚ was quite strong (and we see this across all modules), so it is
probably worthwhile to put in effort during the module.
The assessment‚Äôs deadlines are:
‚ÄĘ Assessment 1: Thursday 20 November 1400 (week 8)
‚ÄĘ Assessment 2: Thursday 04 December 1400 (week 10)
W ork will be submitted via Gradescope. The easiest way to do this is probably via the gradescope mobile
app.
Y ou should contact the module leader if you have marking queries, not the markers directly .
OÔ¨Äice-hours
If you there is something in the course you wish to discuss in detail, such as lecture material you are
struggling with or exercise solutions that do not make sense to you, the place for this is my oÔ¨Äice hours.
They are on T uesdays at 11am . My oÔ¨Äice is 9.10p2 in the Maths Research Deck . T o reach it come
out the front door of the maths department and enter the door into the building opposite that is directly
in front of you, now follow the signs for the Maths Research Deck.
Time management
It is, of course, up to you how you choose to spend your time on this module. But, if you are interested,
my rough recommendation would be something like this (please do not take this too literally , work in a
way that best suits you):
‚ÄĘ Every week: 7.5 hours per week
‚Äď Notes: 2 sections, 45 minutes each
‚Äď Problem sheet: 3 hours per week
8

‚Äď Lecture: 2 hour per week
‚Äď W orkshop: 1 hour per week
‚ÄĘ When required:
‚Äď Assessments 1 and 2: 4 hours and 2 hours
‚Äď Revision : 12 hours
‚ÄĘ T otal: 100 hours
Exam
There will be an exam after the end of the module, making up the remaining 85% of your mark. The
exam will consist of four questions, and you are expected to answer all of them. This will follow a similar
format to the previous (post-Covid) years. In other words it will be an in person exam and NOT open
book. F urther details to follow nearer the time.
Who should I ask about‚Ä¶?
‚ÄĘ I don ‚Äôt understand something in the notes or on a problem sheet : Ask at the beginning of a lecture
or on vevox during lectures, or in a workshop, or in oÔ¨Äice hours.
‚ÄĘ I have an admin question about general arrangements for the module: Email me .
‚ÄĘ I have an admin question about arrangements for my workshop: Email your workshop leader.
‚ÄĘ I have a suggestion for something to cover in the lectures: Email me .
‚ÄĘ I need an extension on or exemption from an assessment: Email the Maths T aught Students OÔ¨Äice .
‚ÄĘ I real ly like this module and want to send compliments: End of module evaluation.
‚ÄĘ I real ly dislike something about this module: Email me as soon as possible in case I can do something
about it. Also end of module evaluation if I fail.
Content of MA TH2702
Prerequisites
It is essential that you are very comfortable with the basics of probability theory: events, probability ,
discrete and continuous random variables, expectation, variance, approximations with the normal distri-
bution, etc. Conditional probability and independence are particularly important concepts in this course.
This course will use the binomial, geometric, Poisson, normal and exponential distributions, although
the notes will usually remind you about them first, in case you have forgotten.
Many students on the module will have studied these topics in MA TH1700 Probability and Statistics;
others will have covered these in different modules.
Syllabus
The course has two major parts: the first part will cover processes in discrete time and the second part
processes in continuous time.
An outline plan of the topics covered is the following. (Remember that one week‚Äôs work is two sections
of notes.)
‚ÄĘ Discrete time Markov chains (+ a bit of martingales) [12 sections]
‚Äď Introduction to stochastic processes [1 section]
‚Äď Important examples: Random walk, gambler‚Äôs ruin, examples from actuarial science and
physics [4 sections]
‚Äď General theory: transition probabilities, ūĚĎõ-step transition probabilities, class structure, period-
icity , hitting times, recurrence and transience, stationary distributions, reversibility , long-term
behaviour [6 sections]
‚Äď Basics of discrete time martingales, optional stopping. Applications to random walks with
boundary [1 section]
‚Äď Revision [1 section]
‚ÄĘ Continuous time Markov jump processes [10 sections]
‚Äď Important examples: Poisson process, counting processes, queues [5 sections]
‚Äď General theory: holding times and jump chains, forward and backward equations, class struc-
ture, hitting times, stationary distributions, reversibility , long-term behaviour [4 sections]
9

‚Äď Revision [1 section]
Books
Y ou can do well on this module by reading the notes, attending the lectures and workshops, and working
on the problem sheets, assignments and practicals, without any further reading. However, students can
benefit from optional extra background reading or an alternative view on the material.
A good book on Markov chains is:
‚ÄĘ J.R. Norris, Markov Chains , Cambridge Series in Statistical and Probabilistic Mathematics, Cam-
bridge University Press, 1997. Chapters 1-3.
This is a whole book just on Markov processes, including some more detailed material that goes beyond
this module. Its coverage of both discrete and continuous time Markov processes is very thorough.
Chapter 1 on discrete time Markov chains is available online.
Other good books covering the material include:
‚ÄĘ G.R. Grimmett and D.R. Stirzaker, Probability and Random Processes , 4th edition, Oxford Uni-
versity Press, 2020. Chapter 6.
‚ÄĘ G. Grimmet and D. W alsh, Probability: an introduction , 2nd edition, Oxford University Press,
2014. Chapter 12.
‚ÄĘ D.R. Stirzaker, Elementary Probability , 2nd edition, Cambridge University Press, 2003. Chapter
9.
Grimmett and Stirzaker is an excellent handbook that covers most of undergraduate probability . The
whole of Stirzaker is available online.
A gentler introduction with plenty of examples is provided by:
‚ÄĘ P .W. Jones and P . Smith, Stochastic Processes: an introduction , 3nd edition, T exts in Statistical
Science, CRC Press, 2018. Chapters 2-7.
Although it does not cover everything in this module. The whole book is available online.
(I have listed the newest editions of these books, but older editions will usually be fine too.)
There are many more beautiful presentations of various aspects of stochastic processes, it is not possible
to give an exhaustive list.
And finally‚Ä¶
These notes were mostly written by Matthew Aldridge in 2018‚Äď19, and have received updates (mostly in
Sections 9‚Äď11) and reformatting in 2020-2021 followed by many small changes by me in the last couple of
years, as well as some big changes in the first half. Some of the material (especially Section 1, Section 6,
and numerous diagrams) follows closely previous notes by Dr Graham Murphy , and also benefited from
reading earlier notes by Dr Robert A ykroyd and Prof Alexander V eretennikov. Dr Aldridges‚Äôs general
help and advice has been very valuable. Many thanks to students in previous runnings of the module
for spotting errors and suggesting improvements.
Part I: Discrete time Markov chains
1 Stochastic processes and the Markov property
‚ÄĘ Stochastic processes with discrete or continuous state space and discrete or continuous time
‚ÄĘ The Markov ‚Äúmemoryless‚ÄĚ property

chunks.content:
1.1 Deterministic and random models
Amodel is an imitation of a real-world system. F or example, you might want to have a model to imitate
the world‚Äôs population, the level of water in a reservoir, cashflows of a pension scheme, or the price of a
stock. Models allow us to try to understand and predict what might happen in the real world in a low
risk, cost effective and fast way .
T o design a model requires a set of assumptions about how it will work and suitable parameters need to
be determined, perhaps based on past collected data.
An important distinction is between deterministic models and random models. Another word for a
random model is a stochastic (‚Äústo -kass -tik ‚ÄĚ) model. Deterministic models do not contain any random
components, so the output is completely determined by the inputs and any parameters. Random models
have variable outcomes to account for uncertainty and unpredictability , so they can be run many times
to give a sense of the range of possible outcomes. If we want to model an extremely complex system we
often will have many uncertainties, meaning that a good stochastic model is the best we can do.
Consider models for:
‚ÄĘ the future position of the Moon as it orbits the Earth,
‚ÄĘ the future price of shares in Apple.
F or the moon, the random components ‚Äď for example, the effect of small meteorites striking the Moon‚Äôs
surface ‚Äď are not very significant and a deterministic model based on physical laws is good enough for
most purposes. F or Apple shares, the price changes from day to day are highly uncertain, so a random
model can account for the variability and unpredictability in a useful way .
1.2 Stochastic processes
If we want to model, for example, the total number of claims to an insurance company in the whole of
2023, we can use a random variable ūĚĎč to model this ‚Äď perhaps a Poisson distribution with an appropriate
mean. However, if we want to track how the number of claims changes over the course of the year 2024,
we will need to use a stochastic process (or ‚Äúrandom process‚ÄĚ).
A stochastic process, which we will usually write as (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ), is an indexed sequence of random variables
that are (usually) dependent on each other.
Each random variable ūĚĎčūĚĎõtakes a value in a state space ūĚíģ which is the set of possible values for
the process. As with usual random variables, the state space ūĚíģ can be discrete or continuous . A
discrete state space denotes a set of distinct possible outcomes, which can be finite or countably infinite.
F or example, ūĚíģ = { Heads,T ails}is the state space for a single coin flip, while in the case of counting
insurance claims, the state space would be the nonnegative integers ūĚíģ = ‚Ą§+= {0,1,2,‚Ä¶} . A continuous
state space denotes an uncountably infinite continuum of gradually varying outcomes. F or example, the
nonnegative real line ūĚíģ = ‚ĄĚ+= {ūĚĎ• ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ ‚ą∂ ūĚĎ• ‚Č• 0} is the state space for the amount of rainfall on a given
day , while some bounded subset of ‚ĄĚ3would be the state space for the position of a gas particle in a box.
F urther, the process has an index set that puts the random variables that make up the process in order.
The index set is usually interpreted as a time variable, telling us when the process will be measured. The
index set for time can also be discrete or continuous. Discrete time denotes a process sampled at distinct
points, often denoted by ūĚĎõ = 0,1,2,‚Ä¶ , while continuous time denotes a process monitored constantly
over time, often denoted by ūĚĎ° ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ+= {ūĚĎ• ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ ‚ą∂ ūĚĎ• ‚Č• 0} . In the insurance example, we might count up
the number of claims each day ‚Äď then the discrete index set will be the days of the year, which we could
denote{1,2,‚Ä¶,365} . Alternatively , we might want to keep a constant tally that we update after every
claim, requiring a continuous time index ūĚĎ°representing time across the whole year. In discrete time, we
can write down the first few steps of the process as (ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,ūĚĎč2,‚Ä¶) .
This gives us four possibilities in total:
‚ÄĘ Discrete time, discrete space
‚Äď Example: Number of students attending each lecture of maths module.
‚Äď Markov chains ‚Äď discrete time, discrete space stochastic processes with a certain ‚ÄúMarkov
property‚ÄĚ ‚Äď are the main topic of the first half of this module.
‚ÄĘ Discrete time, continuous space
‚Äď Example: Daily maximum temperature in Leeds.

metadata.section_title: Stochastic processes and the Markov property

chunks.content:

these are not as important in this course.
‚ÄĘ Continuous time, discrete space
‚Äď Example: Number of visitors to a webpage over time.
‚Äď Markov jump processes ‚Äď continuous time, discrete space stochastic processes with the
‚ÄúMarkov property‚ÄĚ ‚Äď are the main topic of the second half of this module.
‚ÄĘ Continuous time, continuous space
‚Äď Example: Level of the FTSE 100 share index over time.
‚Äď Such processes, especially the famous Brownian motion ‚Äď another process with the Markov
property ‚Äď are very important, but outside the scope of this course. See MA TH3734 Stochastic
Calculus for Finance next year, for example.
1.3 Markov property
Because stochastic processes consist of a large number ‚Äď even uncountably infinitely many ‚Äď random
variables that could all be dependent on each other, they can get extremely complicated. The Markov
property restricts the type of dependencies in a process, to make the process easier to study , but hopefully
leaves most of the useful and interesting examples intact. (Although particular examples of Markov
processes go back further, the first general study was by the Russian mathematician Andrey Andreyevich
Markov , published in 1906.)
Think of a simple board game where we roll dice and move that many squares forward on the board.
Suppose we are currently on the square ūĚĎčūĚĎõ. Then what can we say about which square ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1 we move
to on our next turn?
‚ÄĘūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1 is random, since it depends on the roll of the dice.
‚ÄĘūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1 depends on where we are now ūĚĎčūĚĎõ, since the score of dice will be added onto the number our
current square,
‚ÄĘ Given the square ūĚĎčūĚĎõwhere we are now, ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1 does not depend any further on which sequence of
squares ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąí1 we used to get here.
It is the third point that is the crucial property of the stochastic processes we will study in this course,
and it is called the Markov property or memoryless property . W e say ‚Äúmemoryless‚ÄĚ, because it is
as if the process forgot how it got here ‚Äď we only need to remember what square we have reached, not
which squares we used to get here. The stochastic process before this moment has no bearing on the
future, given where we are now. A mathematical way to say this is that ‚Äúthe past and the future are
conditionally independent given the present. ‚ÄĚ
T o write this down formally , we need to recall conditional probability : the conditional probability of
an event ūĚźī given another event ūĚźĶ is written ‚Ąô(ūĚźī ‚ą£ ūĚźĶ) , and is the probability that ūĚźī occurs given thatūĚźĶ
definitely occurs. Y ou may remember the definition
‚Ąô(ūĚźī ‚ą£ ūĚźĶ) =‚Ąô(ūĚźī‚ą©ūĚźĶ)
‚Ąô(ūĚźĶ),
although is often more useful to reason directly about conditional probabilities than use this formula.
Y ou may also remember that the definition of conditional probability requires that ‚Ąô(ūĚźĶ) > 0 . Whenever
we write down a conditional probability , we implicitly assume the conditioning event has strictly positive
probability without explicitly saying so.
W e also take this opportunity to recall conditional expectation . F or a random variable ūĚĎč taking
values in state space ūĚíģand event ūĚźĶ (with‚Ąô(ūĚźĶ) > 0 ) we define the conditional expectation of ūĚĎč givenūĚźĶ
by
ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚźĶ) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎ†‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎ†‚Ąô(ūĚĎč = ūĚĎ† ‚ą£ ūĚźĶ).
The event ūĚźĶ could take the form {ūĚĎč ‚Č• 6} (assuming ūĚĎč takes values in an appropriate space), so we can
then read the conditional expectation as ‚Äúwhat is the expected value of ūĚĎč, given that it is at least 6‚ÄĚ, or
it could be an event concerning a related random variable etc. Notice that this expectation is a number.
W e can also condition on some other random variable ūĚĎĆ, writing ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) , in this case the conditional
expectation is also a random variable representing our ‚Äúbest guess‚ÄĚ at ūĚĎč given that we know ūĚĎĆ.

expectation ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ = ūĚĎŹ) of the random variable ūĚĎč given the event {ūĚĎĆ = ūĚĎŹ} . W e define a function

The conditional expectation ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) is then the random variable that takes value ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ = ūĚĎŹ) when
ūĚĎĆ = ūĚĎŹ i.e. it is the function ūĚĎď(ūĚĎĆ) . This is best understood with an example.
Example 1.1. LetūĚĎć1,ūĚĎć2be the outcomes of rolling independent fair dice and ūĚĎĆ = ūĚĎć1,ūĚĎč = ūĚĎć1+ ūĚĎć2.
Our best guess at ūĚĎč given we know ūĚĎĆ isūĚĎĆ +3.5 . F ormally we have ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć1+ūĚĎć2‚ą£ ūĚĎć1) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć1‚ą£
ūĚĎć1)+ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć2‚ą£ ūĚĎć1)where we used that conditional expectation is linear (this follows from the linearity of
‚Äúnormal‚ÄĚ expectations).

make a perfect ‚Äúguess‚ÄĚ) or more precisely we see that the function ūĚĎď1(ūĚĎ†) = ūĚĎ† satisfies that ūĚĎď1(ūĚĎ†) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć1‚ą£
ūĚĎć1= ūĚĎ†) .
F orūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć2‚ą£ ūĚĎć1)we note that ūĚĎć1andūĚĎć2are independent so knowing ūĚĎć1does not help us guess the value
ofūĚĎć2and our best guess is simply the expectation ūĚĒľūĚĎć2. Indeed, inserting the definition of conditional
expectation for events we see that ūĚĎď2(ūĚĎ†) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć2)for every ūĚĎ† ‚ąą {1,2,3,4,5,6} satisfies ūĚĎď2(ūĚĎ†) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć2‚ą£
ūĚĎć1= ūĚĎ†) .
Putting this together we have ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) = ūĚĎď1(ūĚĎĆ)+ūĚĎď2(ūĚĎĆ) = ūĚĎĆ +ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎć2) = as claimed.
Another way to think of this is that we can treat ūĚĎĆas a known constant and then compute the conditional
expectation in the same way as we did when conditioning on an event.
Now returning to the Markov property we can make our definition.
Definition 1.1. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ) = (ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,ūĚĎč2,‚Ä¶) be a stochastic process in discrete time ūĚĎõ = 0,1,2,‚Ä¶ and
discrete space ūĚíģ. Then we say that (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)has the Markov property if, for all times ūĚĎõand all states
ūĚĎ•0,ūĚĎ•1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ,ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ąą ūĚíģ we have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąí1= ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ‚ąí1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎ•0) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ).
Here, the left hand side is the probability we go to state ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1 next conditioned on the entire history of
the process, while the right hand side is the probability we go to state ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1 next conditioned only on
where we are now. So the Markov property tells us that it only matters where we are now and not how
we got here.
There is also a similar definition for continuous time processes, which we will come to later in the course.
Stochastic processes that have the Markov property are much easier to study than general processes, as
we only have to keep track of where we are now and we can forget about the entire history that came
before.
In the next section , we will see the first, and most important, example of a discrete time discrete
space Markov chain: the ‚Äúrandom walk‚ÄĚ .

2.1 Simple random walk
Consider the following simple random walk on the integers ‚Ą§: W e start at 0, then at each time step,
we go up by one with probability ūĚĎĚand down by one with probability ūĚĎě = 1 ‚ąí ūĚĎĚ . When ūĚĎĚ = ūĚĎě =1

are equally likely to go up as down, and we call this the simple symmetric random walk .
The simple random walk is a simple but very useful model for lots of processes, like stock prices, sizes of
populations, or positions of gas particles. (In many modern models, however, these have been replaced
by more complicated continuous time and space models.) The simple random walk is sometimes called
the ‚Äúdrunkard‚Äôs walk‚ÄĚ, suggesting it could model a drunk person trying to stagger home.

walk is a discrete time Markov process or Markov chain .

2.3 Exact distribution of the simple random walk

Problem Sheet 1
Y ou should attempt all these questions and write up your solutions in advance of your workshop in week
2 where the answers will be discussed.
1. When designing a model for a quantity that changes over time, one has many decisions to make:
‚ÄĘ Discrete or continuous state space?
‚ÄĘ Discrete or continuous index set for time?
‚ÄĘ Deterministic or stochastic model?
‚ÄĘ If a stochastic model is chosen, is it reasonable to assume that the Markov property holds?
What would you decide for the following scenarios:
(a) The percentage of UK voters with a positive opinion of the Prime Minister in a weekly tracking poll.
(b) The number of points won by a football league club throughout the season.
(c) The temperature of a bowl of water placed in an oven.
(d) The number of people inside the University of Leeds library .
2. A fair six-sided dice is rolled twice, resulting in the values ūĚĎč1,ūĚĎč2‚ąą {1,2,‚Ä¶,6} . LetūĚĎĆ = ūĚĎč1+ ūĚĎč2

(c) the conditional probability ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč1= ūĚĎ• ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ = 9) forūĚĎ• = 1,2,‚Ä¶,6 .
3. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a simple random walk starting from ūĚĎč0= 0 and that at each step goes up one with

4.
(a) LetūĚĎč andūĚĎĆ be independent random variables on discrete state spaces ūĚĎÜūĚĎč andūĚĎÜūĚĎĆ respectively .
Show that ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč) .
(b) LetūĚĎč andūĚĎĆ be random variables (not necessarily independent) on discrete state spaces ūĚĎÜūĚĎčandūĚĎÜūĚĎĆ
respectively . F or a function ūĚĎĒ ‚ą∂ ūĚĎÜūĚĎĆ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ show that ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎĒ(ūĚĎĆ)ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) = ūĚĎĒ(ūĚĎĆ)ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎč ‚ą£ ūĚĎĆ) .

caseūĚĎĚ = ūĚĎě = 1/2 . Using the exact distribution, show that for any ūĚĎé,ūĚĎŹ ‚ąą ‚Ąē we have ‚Ąô(‚ąíūĚĎé ‚Č§ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚Č§ ūĚĎŹ) ‚Üí 0
asūĚĎõ ‚Üí ‚ąě .

increments (ūĚĎćūĚĎõ)have a normal distribution ūĚĎćūĚĎõ‚ąľ N(ūĚúá,ūĚúé2). The model assumes a drift of ūĚúá = 0.7 and a

3 Discrete time Markov chains
‚ÄĘ Definition of time homogeneous discrete time Markov chains
‚ÄĘ Calculating ūĚĎõ-step transition properties
‚ÄĘ The Chapman‚ÄďKolomogorov equations
3.1 Time homogeneous discrete time Markov chains
So far we have seen a a few examples of stochastic processes in discrete time and discrete space with the
Markov property . Now we will develop the theory more generally .
T o define a so-called ‚ÄúMarkov chain‚ÄĚ, we first need to say where we start from, and second what the
probabilities of transitions from one state to another are.
In the simple random walk, we specified the start point ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ exactly , but we could pick the start point
at random according to some distribution ūĚúÜūĚĎĖ= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ) .
After that, we want to know the transition probabilities ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) forūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ . Here,
because of the Markov property , the transition probability only needs to condition on the state we are
in nowūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ , and not on the whole history of the process.
In the case of the simple random walk, for example, we had initial distribution
ūĚúÜūĚĎĖ= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ) = {1 ifūĚĎĖ = 0
0 otherwise
and transition probabilities
‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) =‚éß{
‚é®{‚é©ūĚĎĚ ifūĚĎó = ūĚĎĖ+1
ūĚĎě ifūĚĎó = ūĚĎĖ‚ąí1
0 otherwise.
F or the random walk (and also the gambler‚Äôs ruin which we see next lecture), the transition probabilities
‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) do not depend on ūĚĎõ; in other words, the transition probabilities stay the same
over time. A Markov process with this property is called time homogeneous . W e will always consider
time homogeneous processes from now on (unless we say otherwise).
Let us write ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) for the transition probabilities, which are independent of ūĚĎõ. W e
must have ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó‚Č• 0 , since it is a probability , and we must also have ‚ąĎūĚĎóūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 1 for all states ūĚĎĖ, as this is
the sum of the probabilities of all the places you can move to from state i.
Definition 3.1. Let(ūĚúÜūĚĎĖ)be a probability distribution on a sample space ūĚíģ. LetūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó, whereūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ , be
such that ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó‚Č• 0 for allūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó , and‚ąĎūĚĎóūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 1 for allūĚĎĖ. Let the time index be ūĚĎõ = 0,1,2,‚Ä¶ . Then the time
homogeneous discrete time Markov process or Markov chain (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)with initial distribution (ūĚúÜūĚĎĖ)
and transition probabilities (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó)is defined by
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ) = ūĚúÜūĚĎĖ,
‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąí1= ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ‚ąí1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎ•0) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) = ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó.
When the state space is finite (and even sometimes when it‚Äôs not), it is convenient to write the transition
probabilities (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó)as a matrix P, called the transition matrix , whose (ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó) th entry is ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó. Then the
condition that ‚ąĎūĚĎóūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 1 is the condition that each of the rows of Padd up to 1. A matrix where every
row sums to 1 is also called a (right) stochastic matrix.
Notation: W e will sometimes write ūĚĎĚ(ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó) instead of ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó when it makes things easies to read. F or
example if we consider states ūĚĎ•ūĚĎĀ,ūĚĎ•ūĚĎĀ‚ąí1‚ąą ūĚíģ it is easier to read ūĚĎĚ(ūĚĎ•ūĚĎĀ,ūĚĎ•ūĚĎĀ‚ąí1)thanūĚĎĚūĚĎ•ūĚĎĀūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ‚ąí1.
Example 3.1. In this notation, what is ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ andūĚĎč1= ūĚĎó) ?
First we must start from ūĚĎĖ, and then we must move from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎó, so
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ andūĚĎč1= ūĚĎó) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ)‚Ąô(ūĚĎč1= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ) = ūĚúÜūĚĎĖūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó.

chunks.content:
In this notation, what is ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= ūĚĎó andūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) ?
First we must move from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎė, then we must move from ūĚĎėtoūĚĎó, so
‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= ūĚĎó andūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ)‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė)
= ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎėūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎó.
Note that the term ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė) did not have to depend on ūĚĎčūĚĎõ, thanks to the Markov
property .
3.2 A two-state example
Consider a simple two-state Markov chain with state space ūĚíģ = {0,1} and transition matrix
P= (ūĚĎĚ00ūĚĎĚ01
ūĚĎĚ10ūĚĎĚ11) = (1‚ąíūĚõľ ūĚõľ
ūĚõĹ 1‚ąíūĚõĹ)
for some 0 < ūĚõľ,ūĚõĹ < 1 . Note that the rows of Padd up to 1, as they must.
W e can illustrate Pby a transition diagram , where the blobs are the states and the arrows give the
transition probabilities. (W e don not draw the arrow if ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 0 .) In this case, our transition diagram
looks like this:

Figure 2: T ransition diagram for the two-state Markov chain
W e can use this as a simple model of a broken printer, for example. If the printer is broken (state 0) on
one day , then with probability ūĚõľit will be fixed (state 1) by the next day; while if it is working (state
1), then with probability ūĚõĹit will have broken down (state 0) by the next day .
Example 3.2. If the printer is working on Monday, what is the probability that it also is working on
W ednesday?
If we call Monday day ūĚĎõ, then W ednesday is day ūĚĎõ + 2 , and we want to find the two-step transition
probability .
ūĚĎĚ11(2) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1).
The key to calculating this is to condition on the first step ‚Äď that is, on whether the printer is working
on T uesday . W e have
ūĚĎĚ11(2) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 0 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1)‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 0,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1)
+‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1)‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 1,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1)
= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 0 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1)‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 0)
+‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 1)‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= 1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= 1)
= ūĚĎĚ10ūĚĎĚ01+ūĚĎĚ11ūĚĎĚ11
= ūĚõĹūĚõľ+(1‚ąíūĚõĹ)2.
In the second equality , we used the Markov property to see that conditional probabilities like ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2=
1 ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė) did not have to depend on ūĚĎčūĚĎõ.
This idea of ‚Äúconditioning on the first step‚ÄĚ will be the most crucial tool throughout this whole
module .
Another way to think of this as we summing the probabilities of all length-2 paths from 1 to 1, which
are1 ‚Üí 0 ‚Üí 1 with probability ūĚõĹūĚõľ and1 ‚Üí 1 ‚Üí 1 with probability (1‚ąíūĚõĹ)2.

chunks.content:
3.3 n-step transition probabilities
In the above example, we calculated a two-step transition probability ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(2) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) by
conditioning on the first step. That is, by considering all the possible intermediate steps ūĚĎė, we have
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(2) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎė‚ąąūĚíģ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ)‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+2= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1= ūĚĎė) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎė‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎėūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎó.
But this is exactly the formula for multiplying the matrix Pwith itself! In other words, ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(2) = ‚ąĎūĚĎėūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎėūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎó
is the(ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó) th entry of the matrix square P2= PP . If we write P(2) = (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(2)) for the matrix of two-step
transition probabilities, we have P(2) = P2.
More generally , we see that this rule holds over multiple steps, provided we sum over all the possible
pathsūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎė1‚Üí ūĚĎė2‚Üí ‚čĮ ‚Üí ūĚĎėūĚĎõ‚ąí1‚Üí ūĚĎó of length ūĚĎõfromūĚĎĖtoūĚĎó.
Theorem 3.1. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a Markov chain with state space ūĚíģand transition matrix P= (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó). F or
ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ , write
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ)
for theūĚĎõ-step transition probability. Then
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎė1,ūĚĎė2,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎėūĚĎõ‚ąí1‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė1ūĚĎĚūĚĎė1ūĚĎė2‚čĮūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎõ‚ąí2ūĚĎėūĚĎõ‚ąí1ūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎõ‚ąí1ūĚĎó.
In particular, ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) is the(ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó) th element of the matrix PūĚĎõ, and the matrix of ūĚĎõ-step transition proba-
bilities is given by P(ūĚĎõ) = PūĚĎõ.
The so-called Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov equations follow immediately from this.
Theorem 3.2 (Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov equations) .Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a Markov chain with state space ūĚíģand
transition matrix P= (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó). Then, for non-negative integers ūĚĎõ,ūĚĎö , we have
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎė‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė(ūĚĎõ)ūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎó(ūĚĎö),
or, in matrix notation, P(ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö) = P(ūĚĎõ) P(ūĚĎö) .
In other words, a trip of length ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö fromūĚĎĖtoūĚĎóis a trip of length ūĚĎõfromūĚĎĖto some other state ūĚĎė, then
a trip of length ūĚĎö fromūĚĎėback to ūĚĎó, and this intermediate stop ūĚĎėcan be any state, so we have to sum
the probabilities.
Of course, once we know that P(ūĚĎõ) = PūĚĎõis given by the matrix power, it‚Äôs clear to see that P(ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö) =
PūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö= PūĚĎõPūĚĎö= P(ūĚĎõ) P(ūĚĎö) .
Sydney Chapman (1888‚Äď1970) was a British applied mathematician and physicist, who studied applica-
tions of Markov processes. Andrey Nikolaevich Kolmogorov (1903‚Äď1987) was a Russian mathematician
who did very important work in many different areas of mathematics, is considered the ‚Äúfather of modern
probability theory‚ÄĚ, and studied the theory of Markov processes.

In the next section , we look at a new class of stochastic processes and illustrate their use through a
gambling problem based on the simple random walk.

chunks.content:
4 Martingales and Gambler‚Äôs ruin
‚ÄĘ Martingales in discrete time
‚ÄĘ Application to the Gambler‚Äôs ruin Markov chain
‚ÄĘ Optional stopping theorem
4.1 Martingales
Markov processes are a very important class of stochastic processes but, of course, they do not come
close to encompassing all stochastic processes (or even all the interesting ones). Another interesting class
of stochastic process is the class of martingales (nothing to do with tack for horses). F or a martingale,
the expected value of the process at the next time step given previous values is equal to the most recent
value. Martingales can model ‚Äúfair games‚ÄĚ because given my current total winnings I do not expect to
gain or lose in the next round. In this case my total winnings at each round is a stochastic process that
depends on the game in some way e.g. through a (obviously random) sequence of dice rolls.
Definition 4.1. A discrete time stochastic process (ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ)‚Č•ūĚĎõ on a state space ūĚíģ(not necessarily discrete)
is a martingale with respect to another discrete time stochastic process (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)ūĚĎõ‚Č•0 if, for all ūĚĎõ ‚Č• 0
ūĚĒľ|ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ| < ‚ąě,
ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ.
Notice that if we define ūĚĎćūĚĎõ= (ūĚĎč0,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)thenūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎćūĚĎõ), so this fits
our definition of conditional expectation. W e must remember that conditional expectations are random
variables and so equalities like ūĚĒľ(ūĚźī ‚ą£ ūĚźĶ) = ūĚź∂ need only hold almost surely (i.e. with probability 1).
Example 4.1. Is the simple random walk a martingale? If so, with respect to what process? W ell, we
haveūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎč0+‚ąĎūĚĎõ
ūĚĎĖ=1ūĚĎćūĚĎõso
ūĚĒľ|ūĚĎčūĚĎõ| ‚Č§ |ūĚĎč0|+ūĚĎõ, ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎć1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎćūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+(ūĚĎĚ ‚ąíūĚĎě).
So ifūĚĎĚ = ūĚĎě then(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)is a martingale with respect to (ūĚĎćūĚĎõ), its increments (otherwise it is not). More
generally
ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ= ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąíūĚĎõ(ūĚĎĚ ‚ąíūĚĎě)
is a martingale with respect to (ūĚĎćūĚĎõ)for anyūĚĎĚ ‚ąą [0,1] .
Of course, martingales are not necessarily Markov chains. F or a simple example consider a symmetric
random walk that moves up/down with probability 1/2 except that if it moves upwards or downwards
twice in a row then on the next step it has to stay where it is. Y ou can check this is a martingale but
not a Markov chain (its transitions depend on the previous two steps, not just on the previous step).
It is a common theme that we can find/construct a martingale from a stochastic process by applying the
right function to it (e.g. in the above case the function was ūĚĎď(ūĚĎ•) = ūĚĎ•‚ąíūĚĎõ(ūĚĎĚ ‚ąíūĚĎě) ).
Perhaps our most important example of a martingale comes from the Markov chain known as gambler‚Äôs
ruin .
4.2 Gambler‚Äôs ruin Markov chain
Consider the following game/gambling problem. Alice is gambling against Bob. Alice starts with ¬£ ūĚĎé
and Bob starts with ¬£ ūĚĎŹ. W e write ūĚĎö = ūĚĎé+ūĚĎŹ for the total amount of money , so Bob starts with ¬£ (ūĚĎö‚ąíūĚĎé) .
At each step of the game, both players bet ¬£1; Alice wins ¬£1 off Bob with probability ūĚĎĚ, or Bob wins
¬£1 off Alice with probability ūĚĎě. The game continues until one player is out of money (or is ‚Äúruined‚ÄĚ).
LetūĚĎčūĚĎõdenote how much money Alice has after ūĚĎõsteps of the game. W e can write this as a stochastic
process with discrete time ūĚĎõ ‚ąą {0,1,2,‚Ä¶} = ‚Ą§+and discrete state space ūĚíģ = {0,1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎö} . ThenūĚĎč0= ūĚĎé ,
and, for ūĚĎõ ‚Č• 0 , we have

0 ifūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 0 ,

already over with ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 0 (Alice is ruined) or ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎö (Alice has won all Bob‚Äôs money , so Bob in
ruined).
W e see that the gambler‚Äôs ruin process (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)clearly satisfies the Markov property: the next step ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1
depends on where we are now ūĚĎčūĚĎõ, but, given that, does not depend on how we got here.
The gambler‚Äôs ruin process is exactly like a simple random walk started from ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎé except that we have
absorbing barriers and0andūĚĎö, where the random walk stops because one of the players is ruined.
(One could also consider random walks with reflecting barriers , that bounce the random walk back
into the state space, or mixed barriers that are absorbing or reflecting at random.)
There are two questions about gambler‚Äôs ruin that we will try to answer in this section:
1. What is the probability that the game ends by Alice ruining?
2. How long does the game last on average?
4.3 Probability of ruin
The gambling game continues until either Alice is ruined ( ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 0 ) or Bob is ruined ( ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎö ). A natural
question to ask is: What is the probability that the game ends in Alice‚Äôs ruin?
Let us write ūĚĎüūĚĎĖfor the probability Alice ends up ruined if she currently has ¬£ ūĚĎĖ. Then the probability
of ruin for the whole game is ūĚĎüūĚĎé, since Alice initially starts with ¬£ ūĚĎé. The probability Bob will end up
ruined is 1‚ąíūĚĎüūĚĎé, since one of the players must lose.
What can we say about ūĚĎüūĚĎĖ? Clearly we have ūĚĎü0= 1 , sinceūĚĎčūĚĎõ= 0 means that Alice has run out of money
and is ruined, and ūĚĎüūĚĎö= 0 , sinceūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎö means that Alice has won all the money and Bob is ruined.

when you get there‚ÄĚ .
Definition 4.2. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)ūĚĎõ‚Č•0 be a stochastic process in discrete time, and let ūĚĎábe a random time. Then
ūĚĎáis a stopping time if for all ūĚĎõ, whether or not the event {ūĚĎá = ūĚĎõ} occurs is completely determined by
the random variables ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ.
So, for example:
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThe first visit to state ūĚĎĖ‚ÄĚ is stopping time, because as soon as we reach ūĚĎĖ, we know the value of ūĚĎá.
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThree time-steps after the second visit to ūĚĎó‚ÄĚ is a stopping time, because after our second visit we
count on three more steps and have ūĚĎá.
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThe time-step before the first visit to ūĚĎĖ‚ÄĚ is not a stopping time, because we still need to go one
step further on to know whether we had just been at time ūĚĎá or not.
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThe final visit to ūĚĎó‚ÄĚ is not a stopping time, because at the time of the visit we do not yet know
whether we‚Äôll come back again or not.
There are lots of places in probability theory when something that is true about a fixed time is also true
about a random stopping time.

4.5 The optional stopping theorem
This subsection is nonexaminable.

Theorem 4.3. Let(ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ)ūĚĎõ‚Č•0 be a martingale with respect to (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)‚Č•0 and let T be a stopping time for

ūĚźĺ.

- but we will not need that).
So even if our martingale or stopping rule is very complex we have some criteria for when ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄ0) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄūĚĎá).
F or gambler‚Äôs ruin we construct the martingales from the random walk (although we do not care what
the walk does after we hit 0 or ūĚĎö). W e cannot use condition 1 because, for example, the walk could move
up and down by 1 in a sequence for a long time, so in particular ‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎĀ) ‚Č• 2‚ąíūĚĎĀ> 0 . The random

can use condition 3 if we check ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá) < ‚ąě (because |ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1‚ąíūĚĎčūĚĎõ| = 1 ), but note that for gambler‚Äôs ruin
ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá) is the expected duration of the game! So calculating this expectation a different way would defeat
some of the point of using optional stopping. W e can bypass this this check by tweaking our martingale

5 Examples from actuarial science and some further fun exam-
ples
‚ÄĘ Three Markov chain models for insurance problems
‚ÄĘ A Markov chain from a physical model
‚ÄĘ A martingale produced by a monkey on a typewriter
In this lecture we will set up three simple models for an insurance company that can be analysed using
ideas about Markov chains. The first example has a direct Markov chain model. F or the second and
third examples, we will have to be clever to find a Markov chain associated to the situation.
5.1 A simple no-claims discount model
A motor insurance company puts policy holders into three categories:
‚ÄĘ no discount on premiums (state 1)
‚ÄĘ 25% discount on premiums (state 2)
‚ÄĘ 50% discount on premiums (state 3)
New policy holders start with no discount (state 1). F ollowing a year with no insurance claims, policy
holders move up one level of discount. If they start the year in state 3 and make no claim, they remain
in state 3. F ollowing a year with at least one claim, they move down one level of discount. If they start
the year in state 1 and make at least one claim, they remain in state 1. The insurance company believes
that probability that a motorist has a claim free year is3
4.
W e can model this directly as a Markov chain:
‚ÄĘ the state space ūĚíģ = {1,2,3} is discrete;
‚ÄĘ the time index is discrete, as we have one discount level each year;
‚ÄĘ the probability of being in a certain state at a future time is completely determined by the present
state (the Markov property);
‚ÄĘ the one-step transition probabilities are not time dependent (time homogeneous).
The transition probability and transition diagram of the Markov chain are:

5.2 A fun martingale example
A monkey is left unattended at a computer and starts using the keyboard. They repeatedly type any
of the 26 letters of the English alphabet independently with equal probability 1/26. Let ūĚĎá be the total
number of letters that have been typed when the entire word ‚ÄúABRACADABRA‚ÄĚ first appears. W e want
to findūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá) . Imagine a gambler arrives just before each new letter is typed, they all have the following
strategy . Each gambler first bets ¬£1 on the letter ‚ÄúA‚ÄĚ, if the letter typed is not ‚ÄúA‚ÄĚ the gambler loses
and exits the game. If the letter typed is ‚ÄúA‚ÄĚ the gambler receives ¬£26 and then bets this all on the next
letter being ‚ÄúB‚ÄĚ . If the next letter is not ‚ÄúB‚ÄĚ the gambler loses everything and exits. If the next letter is
‚ÄúB‚ÄĚ they receive ¬£ 262which they bet on the third letter being ‚ÄúR‚ÄĚ . They continue this until they lose or
until ‚ÄúABRACABABRA‚ÄĚ has been typed out, in which case they exit the game with ¬£ 2611andūĚĎá has
been found.
LetūĚĎčūĚĎõbe the total wealth of all gamblers in play after the ūĚĎõūĚĎ°‚Ąéletter has been typed. ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ= ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąí ūĚĎõ
is a martingale. Indeed, while the game is still running, each gambler still in play loses their money
with probability 25/26 and multiplies it by 26 with probability 1/26 and gamblers that have already lost
remain at 0 winnings. So the gambler‚Äôs total wealth does not change except for the new gambler who
brings in ¬£1. Hence ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ+1|ūĚĎč1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎčūĚĎõ+1‚ąí(ūĚĎõ+1) = ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąíūĚĎõ = ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ. AlsoūĚĒľ|ūĚĎÄūĚĎõ| < ‚ąě is easy
to see (it is bounded above by the maximum of ūĚĎõand a finite sum of 26 raised to powers ‚Č§ 11 ). The
stopped martingale ūĚĎÄūĚĎá
ūĚĎõis bounded (by 1+26+262+...+2611) and‚Ąô(ūĚĎá < ‚ąě) = 1 (it is dominated by
a geometric random variable), so we can use optional stopping.
0 = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄ0) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎÄūĚĎá) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎčūĚĎá)‚ąíūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá).
Now here is the key observation: at time ūĚĎá, when the game stops, there are only 3 gamblers in play . The
one who won ¬£ 2611and arrived at ūĚĎá ‚ąí11 , the only who won ¬£ 264by successfully betting on ‚ÄúABRA‚ÄĚ
and arrived at ūĚĎá ‚ąí 4 and the one who just arrived at ūĚĎá and won ¬£26 by successfully betting on ‚ÄúA‚ÄĚ .
Hence
ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá) = ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎčūĚĎá) = ūĚĎčūĚĎá= 2611+264+26 ‚Čą 3.7 ‚ąó1015
(if the monkey types one letter per second this is about 100 million years). Notice that the repetitions
in ‚ÄúABRACABABRA‚ÄĚ led to the terms 264and26 being included in ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá) , increasing it.
5.3 An accident model with memory
Sometimes, we are presented with a situation where the ‚Äúobvious‚ÄĚ stochastic process is not a Markov
chain. But sometimes we can find a related process that is a Markov chain, and study that instead. As
an example of this, we consider at a different accident model.
According to a different model, a motorist‚Äôs ūĚĎõth year of driving is either accident free, or has exactly one
accident. (The model does not allow for more than one accident in a year.) Let ūĚĎĆūĚĎõbe a random variable
so that,
ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ= {0 if the motorist has no accident in year ūĚĎõ,
1 if the motorist has one accident in year ūĚĎõ.
This defines a stochastic process (ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ)with finite state space ūĚíģ = {0,1} and discrete time ūĚĎõ = 1,2,3,‚Ä¶ .
The probability of an accident in year ūĚĎõ + 1 is modelled as a function of the total number of previous
accidents over a function of the number of years in the policy; that is,

5.5 A no-claims discount model with memory
Sometimes, we are presented with a stochastic process which is not a Markov chain, but where by altering
the state space ūĚíģwe can end up with a process which isa Markov chain. As such, when making a model,
it is important to think carefully about choice of state space. T o see this we will return to the no-claims
discount example.
Suppose now we have an model with four levels of discount:
‚ÄĘ no discount (state 1)
‚ÄĘ 20% discount (state 2)
‚ÄĘ 40% discount (state 3)
‚ÄĘ 60% discount (state 4)
If a year is accident free, then the discount increases one level, to a maximum of 60%. This time, if the
year has an accident, then the discount decreases by one level if the year previous to that was accident
free, but decreases by two levels if the previous year had an accident as well, both to a minimum of no
discount.
As before, the insurance company believes that probability that a motorist has a claim-free year is
3
4= 0.75 .

W e might consider the most natural choice of a state space, where the states are discount levels; say ,
ūĚíģ = {1,2,3,4} . But this is not a Markov chain, since if a policy holder has an accident, we may need
to know about the past in order to determine probabilities for future states, which violates the Markov
property . In particular, if a motorist is in state 3 (40% discount) and has an accident, they will either
move down to level 2 (if they had not crashed the previous year, so had previously been in state 2) or
to level 1 (if they had crashed the previous year, so had previously been in state 4) ‚Äď but that depends
on their previous level too, which the Markov property doesn‚Äôt allow. (Y ou can check this is the only
violation of the Markov property .)
However, we can be clever again, this time in the choice of our state space. Instead, we can split the
40% level into two different states: state ‚Äú3a‚ÄĚ if there was no accident the previous year, and state ‚Äú3b‚ÄĚ
if there was an accident the previous year. Our states are now:
‚ÄĘ no discount (state 1)
‚ÄĘ 20% discount (state 2)
‚ÄĘ 40% discount, no claim in previous year (state 3a)
‚ÄĘ 40% discount, claim in previous year (state 3b)
‚ÄĘ 60% discount (state 4)
Now this isa Markov chain, because the new states 3s carry with them the memory of the previous year,
to ensure the Markov property is preserved. Under the assumption of 25% of drivers having an accident
each year, the transition matrix is

6.1 Communicating classes
If we have a large complicated Markov chain, it can be useful to split the state space up into smaller
pieces that can be studied separately . The idea is that states ūĚĎĖandūĚĎóshould definitely be in the same
piece (or ‚Äúclass‚ÄĚ) if we can get from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎóand then back to ūĚĎĖagain after some number of steps.
Definition 6.1. Consider a Markov chain on a state space ūĚíģwith transition matrix P. W e say that
stateūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ isaccessible from state ūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚíģ and write ūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó if, for some ūĚĎõ,ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) > 0 .
IfūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó andūĚĎó ‚Üí ūĚĎĖ , we say that ūĚĎĖcommunicates with ūĚĎóand write ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó .
Here, the condition ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) > 0 for some ūĚĎõmeans that, starting from ūĚĎĖ, there is a positive chance that we
will get to ūĚĎóat some point in the future ‚Äď hence the term ‚Äúaccessible‚ÄĚ .
Theorem 6.1. Consider a Markov chain on a state space ūĚíģ with transition matrix P. Then the
‚Äúcommunicates with‚ÄĚ relation ‚ÜĒ is an equivalence relation ; that is, it has the fol lowing properties:
‚ÄĘ reflexive :ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎĖ for al lūĚĎĖ;
‚ÄĘ symmetric : ifūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó thenūĚĎó ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎĖ ;
‚ÄĘ transitive : ifūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó andūĚĎó ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎė thenūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎė .
Proof. Reflexivity : Clearly ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ(0) = 1 > 0 , because in ‚Äúzero steps‚ÄĚ we stay where we are. So ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎĖ for all
ūĚĎĖ.
Symmetry : The definition of ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó is symmetric under swapping ūĚĎĖandūĚĎó.
T ransitivity . If we can get from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎóand we can get from ūĚĎótoūĚĎė, then we can get from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎėby going
viaūĚĎó. W e just need to write that out formally .
SinceūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó , we have ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) > 0 for some ūĚĎõ, and since ūĚĎó ‚Üí ūĚĎė , we also have ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎė(ūĚĎö) > 0 for some ūĚĎö. Then,
by the Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov equations, we have
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė(ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎô‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎô(ūĚĎõ)ūĚĎĚūĚĎôūĚĎė(ūĚĎö) ‚Č• ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎė(ūĚĎö) > 0,
from just picking out the ūĚĎô = ūĚĎó term in the sum. So ūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎė too.
The same argument with ūĚĎėandūĚĎĖswapped gives ūĚĎė ‚Üí ūĚĎĖ also, soūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎė .
A fact you may remember about equivalence relations is that an equivalence relation, like ‚ÜĒ, partitions
the space ūĚíģinto equivalence classes . This means that each state ūĚĎĖis in exactly one equivalence class,
and that class is the set of states ūĚĎósuch that ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó . In this context, we call these communicating
classes .
Example 6.1. In the simple random walk, provided ūĚĎĚis not 0 or 1, every state communicates with
every other state, because from ūĚĎĖwhen can get to ūĚĎó > ūĚĎĖ by going up ūĚĎó‚ąíūĚĎĖ times, and we can get to ūĚĎó < ūĚĎĖ
by going down ūĚĎĖ‚ąíūĚĎó times. Therefore the whole state space ūĚíģ = ‚Ą§ is one communicating class.
Example 6.2. Consider the gambler‚Äôs ruin Markov chain on {0,1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎö} . There are three communi-
cating classes. The ruin states {0} and{ūĚĎö} each don‚Äôt communicate with any other states, so each are
a class by themselves. The remaining states {1,2,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎö ‚ąí 1} are all in the same class, like the simple
random walk.

A few more definitions that will be important later.
Definition 6.2. If the entire state space ūĚíģis one communicating class, we say that the Markov chain is
irreducible .
W e say that a communicating class is closed if no state outside the class is accessible from any state
within the class. That is, class ūĚź∂ ‚äā ūĚíģ is closed if whenever there exist ūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚź∂ andūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ withūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó , then
ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚź∂ also. If a class is not closed, we say it is open .
If a state ūĚĎĖis in a communicating class {ūĚĎĖ} by itself and that class is closed, then we say state ūĚĎĖis
absorbing .
In non-maths language:
‚ÄĘ An irreducible Markov chain can not be broken down into smaller pieces.
‚ÄĘ Once you enter a closed class, you can not leave that class.
‚ÄĘ Once you reach an absorbing state, you can not leave that state.
How do these work for our earlier examples?
Example 6.4. Going back to the previous examples:
‚ÄĘ In the simple random walk, the whole state space is one communicating class which must therefore
be closed. The Markov chain has only one class, so is irreducible.
‚ÄĘ In the gambler‚Äôs ruin, classes {0} and{ūĚĎö} are closed, because the Markov chain stays there forever,
and because these closed classes consist of only one state each, 0andūĚĎö are absorbing states. The
class{1,2,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎö ‚ąí 1} is open, as we can escape the class by going to 0orūĚĎö. The gambler‚Äôs ruin
chain has multiple classes, so is not irreducible.
‚ÄĘ In the ‚Äúhealthy‚Äďsick‚Äďdead‚ÄĚ chain, the class {ūĚź∑} is closed, so D is an absorbing state, while the
class{ūĚźĽ,ūĚĎÜ} is open, as one can leave it by dying. The Markov chain is not irreducible.
6.2 Periodicity
When we discussed the simple random walk, we noted that it alternates between even-numbered and
odd-numbered states. This ‚Äúperiodic‚ÄĚ behaviour is important to understand if we want to know which
state we will be in at some point in the future.
The idea is this: List the number of steps for all possible paths starting and ending in the state. Then
the period is the greatest common divisor (or ‚Äúhighest common factor‚ÄĚ) of the integers in this list.
Definition 6.3. Consider a Markov chain with transition matrix P. W e say that a state ūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚíģ has
period ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ, where
ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ= gcd{ūĚĎõ ‚ąą {1,2,‚Ä¶,} ‚ą∂ ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎõ) > 0},
where gcd denotes the greatest common divisor.
IfūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ> 1 , then the state ūĚĎĖis called periodic ; ifūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ= 1 , thenūĚĎĖis called aperiodic .
Example 6.5. Consider the simple random walk with ūĚĎĚ ‚Č† 0,1 . W e have ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎõ) = 0 for oddūĚĎõ, since we
swap from odd to even each step. But ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ(2) = 2ūĚĎĚūĚĎě > 0 . Therefore, all states are periodic with period
gcd{2,4,6,‚Ä¶} = 2 .
Example 6.6. F or the gambler‚Äôs ruin, states 0andūĚĎö are aperiodic (have period 1), since they are
absorbing states. The remaining states states 1,2,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎö‚ąí1 are periodic with period 2, because we swap
between odd and even states, as in the simple random walk.

Importantly , we can not return from the triangle side back to the circle side. W e thus see there are two

not irreducible, and there are no absorbing states.

Y ou may have noticed in these examples that, within a communicating class, every state has the same
period. In fact, it is always the case that states in the same class have the same period.
Theorem 6.2. Al l states in a communicating class have the same period.
F ormal ly: Consider a Markov chain on a state space ūĚíģwith transition matrix P. IfūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ are such that
ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó , thenūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ= ūĚĎĎūĚĎó.
In particular, in an irreducible Markov chain, all states have the same period ūĚĎĎ. W e say that an irreducible
Markov chain is periodic ifūĚĎĎ > 1 and aperiodic ifūĚĎĎ = 1 .
Proof. LetūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó be such that ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó . W e want to show that ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ= ūĚĎĎūĚĎó. First we will show that ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ‚Č§ ūĚĎĎūĚĎó, and
then we will show that ūĚĎĎūĚĎó‚Č§ ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ, and thus conclude that they are equal.
SinceūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó , there exist ūĚĎõ,ūĚĎö such that ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) > 0 andūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎö) > 0 . Then, by the Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov
equations,
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎė‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė(ūĚĎõ)ūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎö) ‚Č• ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎö) > 0.
SoūĚĎĎūĚĎĖdividesūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö .
LetūĚĎübe such that ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎó(ūĚĎü) > 0 . Then, by the same Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov argument,
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö+ūĚĎü) ‚Č• ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎó(ūĚĎü)ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎĖ(ūĚĎö) > 0,
because we can get from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎĖby going ūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó ‚Üí ūĚĎó ‚Üí ūĚĎĖ . Hence ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖdividesūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö+ūĚĎü .
But ifūĚĎĎūĚĎĖdivides both ūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö andūĚĎõ+ūĚĎö+ūĚĎü , it must be that ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖdividesūĚĎüalso. So whenever ūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎó(ūĚĎü) > 0 , we
have that ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖdividesūĚĎü. SinceūĚĎĎūĚĎĖis a common divisor of all the ūĚĎü‚Äôs withūĚĎĚūĚĎóūĚĎó(ūĚĎü) > 0 , it can not be any bigger
that the greatest common divisor of all those ūĚĎü‚Äôs. But that greatest common divisor is by definition ūĚĎĎūĚĎó,
the period of ūĚĎó. SoūĚĎĎūĚĎĖ‚Č§ ūĚĎĎūĚĎó.
Repeating the same argument but with ūĚĎĖandūĚĎóswapped over, we get ūĚĎĎūĚĎó‚Č§ ūĚĎĎūĚĎĖtoo, and we are done.
In the next section , we look at two problems to do with ‚Äúhitting times‚ÄĚ: What is the probability we
reach a certain state, and how long on average does it take us to get there?

7.1 Hitting probabilities and expected hitting times
In Section 3 , we used conditioning on the first step to find the ruin probability and expected duration
for the gambler‚Äôs ruin problem. Here, we develop those ideas for general Markov chains.
Definition 7.1. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a Markov chain on state space ūĚíģ. LetūĚźĽūĚźībe a random variable representing
the hitting time to hit the set ūĚźī ‚äā ūĚíģ , given by
ūĚźĽūĚźī= min{ūĚĎõ ‚ąą {0,1,2,‚Ä¶} ‚ą∂ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąą ūĚźī}.
The most common case we will be interested in will be when ūĚźī = {ūĚĎó} is just a single state, so
ūĚźĽūĚĎó= min{ūĚĎõ ‚ąą {0,1,2,‚Ä¶} ‚ą∂ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎó}.
(W e use the convention that ūĚźĽūĚźī= ‚ąě ifūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąČ ūĚźī for allūĚĎõ.)
The hitting probability ‚ĄéūĚĎĖūĚźī of the set ūĚźī starting from state ūĚĎĖis
‚ĄéūĚĎĖūĚźī= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ‚ąą ūĚźī for some ūĚĎõ ‚Č• 0 ‚ą£ ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ) = ‚Ąô(ūĚźĽūĚźī< ‚ąě ‚ą£ ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ).
Again, the most common case of interest is ‚ĄéūĚĎĖūĚĎóthe hitting probability of state ūĚĎóstarting from state ūĚĎĖ.
The expected hitting time ūĚúāūĚĎĖūĚźī of the set ūĚźī starting from state ūĚĎĖis
ūĚúāūĚĎĖūĚźī= ūĚĒľ(ūĚźĽūĚźī‚ą£ ūĚĎč0= ūĚĎĖ).
ClearlyūĚúāūĚĎĖūĚźī can only be finite if ‚ĄéūĚĎĖūĚźī= 1 .
The short summary of this is:
‚ÄĘ The hitting probability ‚ĄéūĚĎĖūĚĎóis the probability we hit state ūĚĎóstarting from state ūĚĎĖ.
‚ÄĘ The expected hitting time ūĚúāūĚĎĖūĚĎóis the expected time until we hit state ūĚĎóstarting from state ūĚĎĖ.

some highly convenient short-cuts.

8.3 Positive and null recurrence
It will be useful later to further divide recurrent classes, where the return probability ūĚĎöūĚĎĖ= 1 , by whether
the expected return time ūĚúáūĚĎĖis finite or not. (Note that transient states always have ūĚúáūĚĎĖ= ‚ąě .)
Definition 8.2. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a Markov chain on a state space ūĚíģ. LetūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚíģ be a recurrent state, so
ūĚĎöūĚĎĖ= 1 , and let ūĚúáūĚĎĖbe the expected return time. If ūĚúáūĚĎĖ< ‚ąě , we say that state ūĚĎĖispositive recurrent ;
ifūĚúáūĚĎĖ= ‚ąě , we say that state ūĚĎĖisnull recurrent .
The following facts can be proven in a similar way to the previous results:
1. In a recurrent class, either all states are positive recurrent or all states are null recurrent.
2. All finite closed classes are positive recurrent.
The first result means we can refer to a ‚Äúpositive recurrent class‚ÄĚ or a ‚Äúnull recurrent class‚ÄĚ, and an
irreducible Markov chain can be a ‚Äúpositive recurrent Markov chain‚ÄĚ or a ‚Äúnull recurrent Markov chain‚ÄĚ .
Putting everything so far together, we have the following classification:
‚ÄĘ open classes are transient;
‚ÄĘ finite closed classes are positive recurrent;
‚ÄĘ infinite closed classes can be positive recurrent, null recurrent, or transient.
W e know that the simple symmetric random walk is recurrent. W e also saw in the last section that
ūĚúáūĚĎĖ= ‚ąě , so it is null recurrent.

8.4 Strong Markov property
This subsection is optional and nonexaminable.
There was a cheat somewhere earlier in this section ‚Äď did you notice it?
The Markov property says that, if at some fixed time ūĚĎõwe have ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ , then the Markov chain from
that point on is just like starting all over again from the state ūĚĎĖ. When we applied this in the proof of
Theorem 8.1 , we were using as ūĚĎõthe first return to state ūĚĎĖ. But that is not a fixed time ‚Äď it‚Äôs a random
time! Did we cheat?
Actually we are fine. The reason is that the first return to ūĚĎĖis not just any old random time, it is a
stopping time, and the Markov property also applies to stopping times too. Recall the definition of
stopping times that we saw in section 4 .
Definition 8.3. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a stochastic process in discrete time, and let ūĚĎá be a random time. Then
ūĚĎáis a stopping time if for all ūĚĎõ, whether or not the event {ūĚĎá = ūĚĎõ} occurs is completely determined by
the random variables ūĚĎč0,ūĚĎč1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎčūĚĎõ.
So, for example:
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThe first visit to state ūĚĎĖ‚ÄĚ is stopping time, because as soon as we reach ūĚĎĖ, we know the value of ūĚĎá.
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThree time-steps after the second visit to ūĚĎó‚ÄĚ is a stopping time, because after our second visit we
count on three more steps and have ūĚĎá.
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThe time-step before the first visit to ūĚĎĖ‚ÄĚ is not a stopping time, because we still need to go one
step further on to know whether we had just been at time ūĚĎá or not.
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄúThe final visit to ūĚĎó‚ÄĚ is not a stopping time, because at the time of the visit we do not yet know
whether we‚Äôll come back again or not.
There are lots of places in probability theory and finance when something that is true about a fixed time
is also true about a random stopping time. When we use the Markov property with a stopping time, we
call it the ‚Äústrong Markov property‚ÄĚ .

9.3 Existence and uniqueness
Given a Markov chain its natural to ask:
1. Does a stationary distribution exist?
2. If a stationary distribution does exists, is there only one, or are there be many stationary distribu-
tions?
The answer is given by the following very important theorem.
Theorem 9.1. Consider an irreducible Markov chain.
‚ÄĘ If the Markov chain is positive recurrent, then a stationary distribution ūĚúčexists, is unique, and is
given by ūĚúčūĚĎĖ= 1/ūĚúáūĚĎĖ, whereūĚúáūĚĎĖis the expected return time to state ūĚĎĖ.
‚ÄĘ If the Markov chain is nul l recurrent or transient, then no stationary distribution exists.
W e give an optional and nonexaminable proof to the first part below .
In our no-claims discount example, the chain is irreducible and, like all finite state irreducible chains, it
is positive recurrent. Thus the stationary distribution ūĚúč = (1
13,3
13,9
13)we found is the unique stationary
distribution for that chain. Once we have the stationary distribution ūĚúč, we get the expected return times
ūĚúáūĚĎĖ= 1/ūĚúčūĚĎĖfor free: the expected return times are ūĚúá1= 13 ,ūĚúá2=13
3= 4.33 , andūĚúá3=13
9= 1.44 .
Note the condition in Theorem 9.1 that the Markov chain is irreducible. What if the Markov chain is
not irreducible, so has more than one communicating class? W e can work out what must happen from
the theorem:
‚ÄĘ If none of the classes are positive recurrent, then no stationary distribution exists.
‚ÄĘ If exactly one of the classes is positive recurrent (and therefore closed), then there exists a unique
stationary distribution, supported only on that closed class.
‚ÄĘ If more the one of the classes are positive recurrent, then many stationary distributions will exist.

9.4 Proof of existence and uniqueness
This subsection is optional and nonexaminable.
It is very important to be able to find the stationary distribution(s) of a Markov chain ‚Äď you can
reasonably expect a question on this to turn up on the exam. Y ou should also know the conditions for
existence and uniqueness of the stationary distribution. Being able to prove existence and uniqueness is
less important, although for completeness we will do so here.
Theorem 9.1 had two points. The more important point was that irreducible, positive recurrent Markov
chains have a stationary distribution, that it is unique, and that it is given by ūĚúčūĚĎĖ= 1/ūĚúáūĚĎĖ. W e give a
proof of that below, doing the existence and uniqueness parts separately . The less important point was
that null recurrent and transitive Markov chains do not have a stationary distribution, and this is more
fiddly . Y ou can find a proof (usually in multiple parts) in books such as Norris, Markov Chains , Section
1.7.
Existence: Every positive recurrent Markov chain has a stationary distribution.

10.2 The detailed balance equations
W e found the transition probabilities and stationary distribution for the reversed chain so now we can
return to our original question: does a Markov chain started from its stationary distribution look the
same run forwards as backwards? It will if the transition probabilities are the same, which leads to the
following definition.
Definition 10.2. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)ūĚĎõ‚ąą‚Ąē be a Markov chain on state space ūĚíģwith transition probabilities ūĚĎĚūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶. If
there is a probability distribution ūĚúčonūĚíģsuch that ūĚĎčūĚĎõandūĚúčsatisfy the detailed balance conditions
ūĚúčūĚĎ•ūĚĎĚūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶= ūĚúčūĚĎ¶ūĚĎĚūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ• for allūĚĎ•,ūĚĎ¶ ‚ąą ūĚíģ
then we say that ūĚĎčūĚĎõisreversible .

F rom the definition we see reversibility of the Markov chain depends on the existence of some ūĚúčsatisfying
detailed balance, could there be many such ūĚúč? The corollory below clarifies the relationship between ūĚúč
andūĚĎčūĚĎõ.
Corollary 10.1. Any distribution ūĚúčthat satisfies the detailed balance conditions for a Markov chain ūĚĎčūĚĎõ
onūĚíģwith transition probabilities ūĚĎĚ(ūĚĎ•,ūĚĎ¶) is a stationary distribution for ūĚĎčūĚĎõ.
The proof of this corollory will be an exercise.
So if we can check that the detailed balance equations hold we can find a stationary distribution. This is
often the easiest way to verify that a distribution ūĚúčis stationary (assuming the stationary distribution
satisfies detailed balance).

10.3 A condition for reversibility
It was mentioned above that checking detailed balance for a candidate stationary distribution ūĚúč(or
solving the detailed balance equations to find ūĚúč) is often an easy was to confirm stationarity . Because
not every Markov chain we encounter is reversible it would be nice to have some criteria to quickly decide
whether or not the chain is reversible, before making the effort to solve the detailed balance equations.
In this section we give one such criteria, first we define the graph of a Markov chain .
Definition 10.3. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)ūĚĎõ‚ąą‚Ąē be a Markov chain on state space ūĚíģ. The graph of ūĚĎčūĚĎõis obtained by
taking the transition diagram of ūĚĎčūĚĎõand forgetting all the directions of edges and removing multiple
edges and self loops.
F or example, if we recall the broken printer example in Section 3 this Markov chain has a graph given
by two vertices joined by a single edge. Our three state non-reversible Markov chain above has a graph
given by a triangle. Recall that a simple (i.e. no multiple edges or self loops) graph is a tree if it has no
cycles.
Lemma 10.1. If the graph of a Markov chain (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)ūĚĎõ‚ąą‚Ąē with stationary distribution ūĚúčis a tree, then it
is reversible.
The proof is non-examinable , here it is.

stationary distribution for ūĚĎčūĚĎõ, we aim to show that ūĚúčsatisfies detailed balance.

andūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ2. Now by stationarity of ūĚúč

11 Long-term behaviour of Markov chains
‚ÄĘ The limit theorem: convergence to the stationary distribution for irreducible, aperiodic, positive
recurrent Markov chains
‚ÄĘ The ergodic theorem for the long-run proportion of time spent in each state
11.1 Convergence to equilibrium
In this section we are interested in what happens to a Markov chain (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)in the long-run ‚Äď that is, when
ūĚĎõtends to infinity .
One thing that could happen over time is that the distribution ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) of the Markov chain could grad-
ually settle down towards some ‚Äúequilibrium‚ÄĚ distribution. F urther, perhaps that long-term equilibrium
might not depend on the initial distribution, but the effects of the initial distribution might eventually
almost disappear, exhibiting a ‚Äúlack of memory‚ÄĚ of the start of the process.
Just in case that does happen, let us give it a name.
Definition 11.1. Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be a Markov chain on a state space ūĚíģwith transition matrix P. Suppose
there exists a distribution p‚ąó= (ūĚĎĚ‚ąó

ūĚĎóasūĚĎõ ‚Üí ‚ąě for allūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ . Then we say that p‚ąóis an
equilibrium distribution .
It is clear there can only be at most one equilibrium distribution ‚Äď but will there be one at all? The
following is the most important result in this course. (Recall that an irreducible Markov chain is aperiodic
if it has period 1.)
Theorem 11.1 (Limit theorem) .Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be an irreducible and aperiodic Markov chain. Then for any
initial distribution ūĚúÜ, we have that ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎó) ‚Üí 1/ūĚúáūĚĎóasūĚĎõ ‚Üí ‚ąě , where ūĚúáūĚĎóis the expected return time
to state ūĚĎó.
In particular:
‚ÄĘ Suppose (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)is positive recurrent. Then the unique stationary distribution ūĚúčgiven by ūĚúčūĚĎó= 1/ūĚúáūĚĎó

This theorem gathers together all the ideas from the course in one result (Markov chains, irreducibility ,
periodicity , recurrence/transience, positive/null recurrence, return times, stationary distribution‚Ä¶).
Note the three conditions for convergence to an equilibrium distribution: irreducibility , aperiodicity , and
positive recurrence.

11.3 Ergodic theorem
The limit theorem looked at the limit of ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎó) , the probability that the Markov chain is in state ūĚĎó
at some specific point in time ūĚĎõa long time in the future. W e could also look at the long-run amount of
time spent in state ūĚĎó; that is, averaging the behaviour over a long time period. (The word ‚Äúergodic‚ÄĚ is
used in mathematics to refer to concepts to do with the long-term proportion of time.)
Let us write
ūĚĎČūĚĎó(ūĚĎĀ) ‚ą∂= #{ūĚĎõ < ūĚĎĀ ‚ą∂ ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎó}
for the total number of visits to state ūĚĎóup to time ūĚĎĀ. Then we can interpret ūĚĎČūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)/ūĚĎõ as the proportion
of time up to time ūĚĎõspent in state ūĚĎó, and its limiting value (if it exists) to be the long-run proportion
of time spent in state ūĚĎó.
Theorem 11.3 (Ergodic theorem) .Let(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)be an irreducible Markov chain. Then for any initial
distribution ūĚúÜwe have that ūĚĎČūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)/ūĚĎõ ‚Üí 1/ūĚúáūĚĎóalmost surely as ūĚĎõ ‚Üí ‚ąě , where ūĚúáūĚĎóis the expected return
time to state ūĚĎó.
In particular:
‚ÄĘ Suppose (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)is positive recurrent. Then there is a unique stationary distribution ūĚúč given by
ūĚúčūĚĎó= 1/ūĚúáūĚĎó, andūĚĎČūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)/ūĚĎõ ‚Üí ūĚúčūĚĎóalmost surely for al l ūĚĎó.
‚ÄĘ Suppose (ūĚĎčūĚĎõ)is nul l recurrent or transient. Then ūĚĎČūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)/ūĚĎõ ‚Üí 0 almost surely for al l ūĚĎó.
F or completeness, we should note that ‚Äúalmost sure‚ÄĚ convergence means that ‚Ąô(ūĚĎČūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ)/ūĚĎõ ‚Üí 1/ūĚúáūĚĎó) = 1 ,
although the precise definition is not important for us in this module.
Note that, because we are averaging over a long-time period, we no longer need the condition that
the Markov chain is aperiodic; for convergence of the long-term proportion of time to the stationary
distribution we just need irreducibility and positive recurrence.
Again, we give an optional and nonexaminable proof below .

11.4 Proofs of the limit and ergodic theorems
This subsection is optional and nonexaminable.
Again, it is important to be able to use the limit and ergodic theorems, but less important to be able to
prove them.
First, the limit theorem. The only bit left is the first part: that for an irreducible, aperiodic, positive
recurrent Markov chain, the stationary distribution ūĚúčis an equilibrium distribution.
This cunning proof uses a technique called ‚Äúcoupling‚ÄĚ . When looking at two different random objects
ūĚĎč andūĚĎĆ (like random variables or stochastic processes), it seems natural to prefer ūĚĎč andūĚĎĆ to be
independent. However, coupling is the idea that it can sometimes be beneficial to let ūĚĎč andūĚĎĆ actually
be dependent on each other.

distribution for ever.

13.1 Poisson distribution
In the next three sections we will be considering the Poisson process, a continuous time discrete space
process with the Markov property . Given its name, it is not surprising to hear the Poisson process is
related to the Poisson distribution . Let us start with a reminder of that.
Recall that a discrete random variable ūĚĎč has a Poisson distribution with rate ūĚúÜ, written ūĚĎč ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúÜ) ,
if its probability mass function is
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč = ūĚĎõ) = e‚ąíūĚúÜūĚúÜūĚĎõ
ūĚĎõ!ūĚĎõ = 0,1,2,‚Ä¶.
The Poisson distribution is often used to model the number of ‚Äúarrivals‚ÄĚ in a fixed amount of time ‚Äď for
example, the number of calls to a call centre in one hour, the number of claims to an insurance company
in one year, or the number of particles decaying from a large amount of radioactive material in one
second.
The Poisson distribution is named after the F rench mathematician and physicist Sim√©on Denis Poisson,
who studied it in 1837, although it was used by another F rench mathematician, Abraham de Moivre,
more than 100 years earlier.
Recall the following facts about of the Poisson distribution:
1. Its expectation is ūĚĒľūĚĎč = ūĚúÜ and the variance is Var (ūĚĎč) = ūĚúÜ .
2. IfūĚĎč ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúÜ) andūĚĎĆ ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúá) are independent, then ūĚĎč +ūĚĎĆ ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá) .
3. LetūĚĎč ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúÜ) represent some arrivals, and independently ‚Äúmark‚ÄĚ each arrival with probability
ūĚĎĚ. Then the number of marked arrivals ūĚĎĆ has distribution ūĚĎĆ = Po(ūĚĎĚūĚúÜ) , the number of unmarked
arrivalsūĚĎć has distribution ūĚĎć ‚ąľ Po((1‚ąíūĚĎĚ)ūĚúÜ) , andūĚĎĆ andūĚĎć are independent.
(I say ‚Äúrecall‚ÄĚ, but it is OK if the third one is new to you.)
13.2 Definition 1: Poisson increments
Suppose that, instead of just modelling the number of arrivals in a fixed amount of time, we want to
continually model the total number of arrivals as it changes over time. This will be a stochastic process
with discrete state space ūĚíģ = ‚Ą§+= {0,1,2,‚Ä¶} and continuous time ‚ĄĚ+= [0,‚ąě) . In continuous time,
we will normally write stochastic processes as (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) , with the time variable being a ūĚĎ°in brackets, rather
than a subscript ūĚĎõas we had in discrete time.
Suppose calls arrive at a call centre at a rate of ūĚúÜ = 100 an hour. The following assumptions seem
reasonable:
‚ÄĘ W e begin counting with ūĚĎč(0) = 0 calls.
‚ÄĘ The number of calls in the first hour is ūĚĎč(1) ‚ąľ Po(100) . The number of calls in the second hour
ūĚĎč(2)‚ąíūĚĎč(1) will also be Po (100) , and will be independent of the number of calls in the first hour.
‚ÄĘ The number of calls in a two hour period will be ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+2)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) ‚ąľ Po(200) , while the number of
calls in a half-hour period will be ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+1
2)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) ‚ąľ Po(50) .
These properties will define the Poisson process.
Definition 13.1. The Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜis defined as followed. It is a stochastic process
(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) with continuous time ūĚĎ° ‚ąą [0,‚ąě) and discrete state space ūĚíģ = ‚Ą§+with the following properties:
1.ūĚĎč(0) = 0 ;
2. Poisson increments: ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ†)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúÜūĚĎ†) for allūĚĎ†,ūĚĎ° > 0 ;
3. independent increments: ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°2)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1)andūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°4)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°3)are independent for all ūĚĎ°1‚Č§ ūĚĎ°2‚Č§ ūĚĎ°3‚Č§ ūĚĎ°4.
Note that the condition ūĚĎ°1‚Č§ ūĚĎ°2‚Č§ ūĚĎ°3‚Č§ ūĚĎ°4means that the time interval from ūĚĎ°1toūĚĎ°2and the time
interval from ūĚĎ°3toūĚĎ°4do not overlap. (Overlapping time intervals will not have independent increments,
as arrivals in the overlap will count for both.)
The Poisson process was discovered in the first decade 20th century , and the process was named after
the distribution. Many people were working on similar things, so it is diÔ¨Äicult to say who discovered it
first, but important work was done by the Swedish actuary and mathematician Filip Lundberg and the
Danish engineer and mathematician AK Erlang.
