14.1 Exponential distribution
Last time, we introduced the Poisson process by looking at the random number of arrivals in fixed
amount of time, which follows a Poisson distribution. Another way of looking at the Poisson process is
to look at the random amount of of time required for a fixed number of arrivals. F or this, we will need
the exponential distribution.
W e start by recalling the exponential distribution. Recall that we say a continuous random variable ūĚĎáhas
the exponential distribution with rate ūĚúÜ, and write ūĚĎá ‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜ) , if it has the probability distribution
function ūĚĎď(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚúÜūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°forūĚĎ° ‚Č• 0 .
Exponential distributions are often used to model ‚Äúwaiting times‚ÄĚ ‚Äď for example, the amount of time
until a light bulb breaks, the times between buses arriving, or the time between withdrawals from a bank
account.
Recall the following facts about the exponential distribution:
‚ÄĘ The cumulative distribution function is ūĚźĻ(ūĚĎ°) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎá ‚Č§ ūĚĎ°) = 1 ‚ąí ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°; although it is usually more
convenient to deal with the tail probability ‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°) = 1‚ąíūĚźĻ(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°.
‚ÄĘ The expectation is ūĚĒľūĚĎá = 1/ūĚúÜ and the variance is Var (ūĚĎá) = 1/ūĚúÜ2.
The following memoryless property will of course be important in a course about memoryless Markov
processes.
Theorem 14.1. LetūĚĎá ‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜ) . Then, for any ūĚĎ†,ūĚĎ° ‚Č• 0 ,
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ† ‚ą£ ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ†).
Suppose we are waiting an exponentially distributed time for an alarm to go off. No matter how long
we have been waiting for, the remaining time to wait is still exponentially distributed, with the same
parameter ‚Äď hence ‚Äúmemoryless‚ÄĚ .
Proof. By standard use of conditional probability , we have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ† ‚ą£ ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°) =‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ† andūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°)
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°)=‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ†)
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚĎ°)=ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜ(ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ†)
ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°= ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ†,
which is still the tail probability of the exponential distribution.
The following property will be important later on in the course.
Theorem 14.2. LetūĚĎá1‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜ1),ūĚĎá2‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜ2),‚Ä¶,ūĚĎáūĚĎõ‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜūĚĎõ)be independent exponential
distributions, and let ūĚĎá be the minimum of the ūĚĎáūĚĎĖ‚Äôs, soūĚĎá = min{ūĚĎá1,ūĚĎá2,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎáūĚĎõ}. Then
ūĚĎá ‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜ1+ūĚúÜ2+‚čĮ+ūĚúÜūĚĎõ).
F urther, the probability that ūĚĎáūĚĎóis the smal lest of al l the ūĚĎáūĚĎĖ‚Äôs is
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá = ūĚĎáūĚĎó) =ūĚúÜūĚĎó
ūĚúÜ1+ūĚúÜ2+‚čĮ+ūĚúÜūĚĎõ.
Proof. The proof of this is an question on Problem Sheet 7 .
72

metadata.section_title: Poisson process with exponential holding times

chunks.content:
14.2 Definition 2: exponential holding times
W e mentioned before that exponential distributions are often used to model ‚Äúwaiting times‚ÄĚ . When
modelling a process (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) counting many arrivals at rate ūĚúÜ, we might model the process like this: after
waiting an Exp (ūĚúÜ) amount of time, an arrival appears. After another Exp (ūĚúÜ) amount of time, another
arrival appears. And so on. W e often use the term ‚Äúholding time‚ÄĚ to refer to the time between consecutive
arrivals.
This suggests a process with the following properties:
‚ÄĘ W e start with ūĚĎč(0) = 0 .
‚ÄĘ LetūĚĎá1,ūĚĎá2,‚čĮ ‚ąľ Exp(ūĚúÜ) be the holding times, all independent. Then
ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) =‚éß{{
‚é®{{‚é©0 for0 ‚Č§ ūĚĎ° < ūĚĎá1
1 forūĚĎá1‚Č§ ūĚĎ° < ūĚĎá1+ūĚĎá2
2 forūĚĎá1+ūĚĎá2‚Č§ ūĚĎ° < ūĚĎá1+ūĚĎá2+ūĚĎá3
and so on.
A process described like this is also the Poisson process!
Theorem 14.3. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜ, as defined by its independent Poisson
increments (see Subsection 13.2 ). Then (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) has the exponential holding times structure described
above.
Proof. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜ. W e seek the distribution of the first arrival time ūĚĎá1.
W e have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá1> ūĚĎ°1) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1)‚ąíūĚĎč(0) = 0) = ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°1(ūĚúÜūĚĎ°1)0
0!= ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°1,
since the arrival comes after ūĚĎ°1if there are no arrivals in the interval [0,ūĚĎ°1]. But this is precisely the tail
probability of an exponential distribution with rate ūĚúÜ.
Now consider the second holding time. W e have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá2> ūĚĎ°2‚ą£ ūĚĎá1= ūĚĎ°1) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1+ūĚĎ°2)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1) = 0) = ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜ((ūĚĎ°1+ūĚĎ°2)‚ąíūĚĎ°1)= ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°2
by the same argument as before. This is the tail distribution for another Exp (ūĚúÜ) distribution, independent
ofūĚĎá1.
Repeating this argument for all ūĚĎõgives the desired exponential holding time structure.
In the proof we conditioned on the event {ūĚĎá1= ūĚĎ°1}which has probability 0. W e are technically not
allowed to do this (although intuitively we see what the conditional probability must mean). T o fill this
gap we condition on the event ūĚĎá1‚ąą (ūĚĎ°1‚ąíūĚúÄ,ūĚĎ°1+ūĚúÄ) . W e have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá2> ūĚĎ°2‚ą£ ūĚĎá1= ūĚĎ°1) = lim
ūĚúÄ‚Üí0‚Ąô(ūĚĎá2> ūĚĎ°2‚ą£ ūĚĎá1‚ąą (ūĚĎ°1‚ąíūĚúÄ,ūĚĎ°1+ūĚúÄ)) ‚Č§ lim
ūĚúÄ‚Üí0‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1+ūĚĎ°2+ūĚúÄ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1‚ąíūĚúÄ) = 0)
= lim
ūĚúÄ‚Üí0ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜ(ūĚĎ°1+2ūĚúÄ)= ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°1
and similarly we have ‚Ąô(ūĚĎá2> ūĚĎ°2‚ą£ ūĚĎá1= ūĚĎ°1) ‚Č• limūĚúÄ‚Üí0ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜ(ūĚĎ°1‚ąí2ūĚúÄ)= ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎ°1which fills this gap.
Example 14.1. Customers visit a second-hand bookshop at a rate of ūĚúÜ = 5 per hour. Each customer
buys a book with probability ūĚĎĚ = 0.4 . What is the expected time to make ten sales, and what is the
standard deviation?
The count of books sold is a marked Poisson process, which we saw last time is itself a Poisson process
with rate ūĚĎĚūĚúÜ = 2 .
The expected time of the tenth sale is
ūĚĒľ(ūĚĎá1+‚čĮ+ūĚĎá10) = 10ūĚĒľūĚĎá1= 10√ó1
2= 5 hours.
The variance is
Var(ūĚĎá1+‚čĮ+ūĚĎá10) = 10 Var(ūĚĎá1) = 10√ó1
22= 2.5,
73

metadata.section_title: Poisson process with exponential holding times

chunks.content:
where we used that the holding times are independent and identically distributed, so the standard
deviation is‚ąö
2.5 = 1.58 hours.
What is the probability it takes more than an hour to sel l the first book?
This is the probability the first holding time is longer than an hour, which is the tail of the exponential
distribution,
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá1> 1) = ūĚĎí‚ąí2√ó1= ūĚĎí‚ąí2= 0.135.
An alternative way to solve this would be to say that the first book is is sold later than 1 hour in if
ūĚĎč(1) = 0 . So using the Poisson increments definition from last time, we have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(1) = 0) = ūĚĎí‚ąí2√ó1(2√ó1)0
0!= ūĚĎí‚ąí2= 0.135.
W e get the same answer either way .
14.3 Markov property in continuous time
W e previously saw the Markov ‚Äúmemoryless‚ÄĚ property in discrete time. The equivalent definition in
continuous time is the following.
Definition 14.1. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a stochastic process on a discrete state space ūĚíģand continuous time
ūĚĎ° ‚ąą [0,‚ąě) . W e say that (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) has the Markov property if
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1) = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1) = ūĚĎ•1,ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°0) = ūĚĎ•0)
= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1) = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ)
for all times ūĚĎ°0< ūĚĎ°1< ‚čĮ < ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ< ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1 and all states ūĚĎ•0,ūĚĎ•1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ,ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ąą ūĚíģ .
In other words, the state of the process at some point ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1 in the future depends on where we are now
ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ, but, given that, does not depend on any collection of previous times ūĚĎ°0,ūĚĎ°1,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ‚ąí1 .
The Poisson process does indeed have the Markov property , since, by the property of independent incre-
ments, we have that
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1) = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ą£ ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ,‚Ä¶,ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°0) = ūĚĎ•0) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ) = ūĚĎ•),
whereūĚĎ• = ūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ+1‚ąíūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ. By Poisson increments, this has probability
ūĚĎí‚ąíūĚúÜ(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1‚ąíūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ)(ūĚúÜ(ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ+1‚ąíūĚĎ°ūĚĎõ))ūĚĎ•
ūĚĎ•!.
Alternatively , by the memoryless property of the exponential distribution, we see that we can restart the
holding time from ūĚĎ°ūĚĎõand still have this and all future holding times exponentially distributed.
In the next section , we look at the Poisson process in a third way , by looking at what happens in a
very small time period.

metadata.section_title: Poisson process with exponential holding times

chunks.content:
15 Poisson process in infinitesimal time periods
‚ÄĘ Poisson process in terms of increments in infinitesimal time
‚ÄĘ F orward equations for the Poisson process
15.1 Definition 3: increments in infinitesimal time
W e have seen two definitions of the Poisson process so far: one in terms of increments having a Poisson
distribution, and one in terms of holding times having an exponential distribution. Here we will see
another definition, by looking at what happens in a very small time period of length ūĚúŹ. Advantages
of this approach include that it does not require direct assumptions on the distributions involved, so
may seem more ‚Äúnatural‚ÄĚ or ‚Äúinevitable‚ÄĚ . It also shows links between Markov processes and differential
equations. A disadvantage is that it is normally easier to do calculations with the Poisson or exponential
definitions.
Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a stochastic processes counting some arrivals over time. Consider the number of arrivals in
a very small time period of length ūĚúŹ; that number of arrivals is ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) . The following (seemingly)
weak assumptions seem justified:
‚ÄĘ It is quite likely that there will be no arrivals in the very small time period.
‚ÄĘ It is possible there will be one arrival, and that probability will be roughly proportional to the
lengthūĚúŹof the time period.
‚ÄĘ It is extremely unlikely there will be two or more arrivals in such a short time period.
T o write this down formally in maths, we will consider ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ° + ūĚúŹ) ‚ąí ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎó) as the length ūĚúŹof the
time period tends to zero.
It will be helpful for us to use ‚Äúlittle- ūĚĎú‚ÄĚ notation. Here ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) means a term that is of ‚Äúlower order‚ÄĚ
compared to ūĚúŹ. These are terms like ūĚúŹ2orūĚúŹ3that are so tiny that we are safe to ignore them. F ormally ,
ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) stands for a function of ūĚúŹthat tends to 0asūĚúŹ ‚Üí 0 even when divided by ūĚúŹ. That is,
ūĚĎď(ūĚúŹ) = ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) ‚üļūĚĎď(ūĚúŹ)
ūĚúŹ‚Üí 0 asūĚúŹ ‚Üí 0 .
Then our suggestions above translate to
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎó) =‚éß{
‚é®{‚é©1‚ąíūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) ifūĚĎó = 0 ,
ūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) ifūĚĎó = 1 ,
ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) ifūĚĎó ‚Č• 2 .(11)
asūĚúŹ ‚Üí 0 .
It turns out that we have once again defined the Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜ.
Theorem 15.1. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a stochastic process with the fol lowing properties:
1.ūĚĎč(0) = 0 ;
2. infinitesimal increments: ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) has the structure in (11)above, as ūĚúŹ ‚Üí 0 ;
3. independent increments: ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°2)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°1)andūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°4)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°3)are independent for al l ūĚĎ°1‚Č§ ūĚĎ°2‚Č§ ūĚĎ°3‚Č§ ūĚĎ°4.
Then(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) is a Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜ.
W e will prove this in a moment. First let us see an example of its use.
15.2 Example: sum of two Poisson processes
W e mentioned before the sum of two Poisson processes : that if (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) is a Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜ
and(ūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°)) is an independent Poisson process with rate ūĚúá, then the sum (ūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°)) whereūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)+ūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°)
is a Poisson process with rate ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá .
On Problem Sheet 7 , Question 3, you proved this from the Poisson increments definition. But it is
perhaps even easier to prove using the infinitesimals definition.
76

W e have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°) = 0) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 0)‚Ąô(ūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°) = 0)
= (1‚ąíūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))(1‚ąíūĚúáūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))
= 1‚ąí(ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá)ūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ),
sinceūĚĎć does not increase provided neither ūĚĎč norūĚĎĆ increases.
W e also have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°) = 1)
= ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 1)‚Ąô(ūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°) = 0)
+‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 0)‚Ąô(ūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎĆ(ūĚĎ°) = 1)
= (ūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))(1‚ąíūĚúáūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))+(1‚ąíūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))(ūĚúáūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))
= (ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá)ūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ),
sinceūĚĎć increases by 1 if either ūĚĎč increases by 1 and ūĚĎĆ stays fixed or vice versa.
Finally , since probabilities must add up to 1, we must have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎć(ūĚĎ°) ‚Č• 2) = ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ).
But what we have written down is precisely the infinitesimals definition of a Poisson process with rate
ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá , so we are done.
15.3 F orward equations and proof of equivalence
If we compare Theorem 15.1 to the definition of the Poisson process in terms of Poisson increments, we
see that properties 1 and 3 are the same. W e only need to check property 2, that ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ ūĚĎ†) ‚ąí ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ†) is a
Poisson distribution with rate ūĚúÜūĚĎ°.
Since the increments as defined in ( 11) are time homogeneous, it will suÔ¨Äice to consider ūĚĎ† = 0 . So we
need to show that ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) ‚ąľ Po(ūĚúÜūĚĎ°) .
W riteūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎó) . Then for ūĚĎó ‚Č• 1 we have
ūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ) = (1‚ąíūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))ūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)+(ūĚúÜūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))ūĚĎĚūĚĎó‚ąí1(ūĚĎ°)+ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ),
since we get to ūĚĎóeither by staying at ūĚĎóor by moving up from ūĚĎó ‚ąí 1 , with all other highly unlikely
possibilities being absorbed into the ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) . In order to deal with increments, it will be convenient to take
aūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) to the left-hand side and rearrange, to get the increment
ūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) = ‚ąíūĚúÜūĚúŹūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)+ūĚúÜūĚúŹūĚĎĚūĚĎó‚ąí1(ūĚúŹ)+ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ),
where ‚Äúconstant times ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) ‚ÄĚ is itself ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) .
The only way to deal with the ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) term, given its definition, is to divide everything by ūĚúŹand send ūĚúŹ ‚Üí 0 .
Dividing by ūĚúŹgives
ūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ)‚ąíūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)
ūĚúŹ= ‚ąíūĚúÜūĚĎĚūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)+ūĚúÜūĚĎĚūĚĎó‚ąí1(ūĚĎ°)+ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ)
ūĚúŹ.
Sending ūĚúŹto 0, the term ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ)/ūĚúŹ tends to 0 and vanishes, while we recognise the limit of the left-hand

16 Counting processes
‚ÄĘ Birth processes, including the simple birth process
‚ÄĘ Time inhomogeneous Poisson processes
16.1 Birth processes
The Poisson process was made easier to understand because it was a counting process . That is,
because it was counting arrivals, we always knew that the next change would be an increase by 1; the
only question was when that increase would happen.
In this section, we look at two other types of counting process; the only transitions will still only ever be
fromūĚĎĖtoūĚĎĖ+1 , but the holding times will not just be IID exponential distributions any more.
W e start with the simple birth process , which can model, for example, the division of cells in a
biological experiment. W e suppose that each individual has an offspring (eg the cell divides) after an
Exp(ūĚúÜ) period of time, and continues to have more offspring after another Exp (ūĚúÜ) period of time, and
so on.
W e start with ūĚĎč(0) = 1 individual. After an Exp (ūĚúÜ) time, the individual has an offspring, and we have
ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 2 .
Now each of these two individuals will have an offspring after an Exp (ūĚúÜ) time each. So how much longer
will it be until the first offspring appears and we get ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 3 ? W ell, the earliest offspring will appear
at the minimum of these two Exp (ūĚúÜ) times, and we saw in the Section 14 that this has an Exp (2ūĚúÜ)
distribution. (Remember that the mean of an exponential distribution is the reciprocal of the rate
parameter, so the holding time for the second offspring is half that of the first, on average.)
Now suppose we have ūĚĎõindividuals. By the memoryless property of the exponential distribution, they
each still have an Exp (ūĚúÜ) time until producing an offspring, so the time until the next offspring is the
minimum of these, which is Exp (ūĚĎõūĚúÜ) .
In general, we have built a counting process defined entirely by its starting point ūĚĎč(0) = 1 , that the ūĚĎõth
holding time ūĚĎáūĚĎõis exponential with rate ūĚĎõūĚúÜ , and that all transitions are increases by 1.
On Problem Sheet 8 , you will show that the expected size of the population of the simple birth process
at timeūĚĎ°isūĚĒľūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = eūĚúÜūĚĎ°, so the population grows exponentially quickly (on average).
The simple birth process is an example of the more general class of birth processes.

16.2 Time inhomogeneous Poisson process
When we dealt with the Poisson process, the rate of arrivals ūĚúÜwas the same all the time ‚Äď so our call
centre always received calls at the same rate, or the insurance company always received claims at the
same rate. In real life, though, the rate of arrivals might be lower at some times and higher at some
other times, so the rate ūĚúÜ = ūĚúÜ(ūĚĎ°) should perhaps depend on the time ūĚĎ°.
This gives a process that is time inhomogeneous . The normal Poisson process and the birth processes
were all time homogeneous ; that is, the transition probabilities ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚĎ†) = ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎĖ) depended

we consider in the rest of this course will be time homogeneous, but this subsection is the exception,
because here the transition probabilities will change over time.

17 Continuous time Markov jump processes
‚ÄĘ Time homogeneous continuous time Markov jump processes in terms of the jump Markov chain

‚ÄĘ Explosion of Markov jump processes
17.1 Jump chain and holding times
In this section, we will start looking at general Markov processes in continuous time and discrete space.
All our examples will be time homogeneous, in that the transition probabilities and transition rates will
remain constant over time.
W e call these Markov jump processes , as we will wait in a state for a random holding time, and then
we will suddenly ‚Äújump‚ÄĚ to another state.
The idea will be to treat two matters separately:
‚ÄĘ where we jump too: This will be studied through the ‚Äújump chain‚ÄĚ, a discrete time Markov
chain that tells us what transitions are made (but not when they are made).
‚ÄĘ how long we wait until we jump: we call these the ‚Äúholding times‚ÄĚ . T o preserve the Markov
property , these holding times must have an exponential distribution, since this is the only random
variable that has the memoryless property .
Let us consider a Markov jump process (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) on a state space ūĚíģ. Suppose we are at a state ūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚíģ . The
transition rate at which we wish to jump to a state ūĚĎó ‚Č† ūĚĎĖ will be written ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó‚Č• 0 . This means that,
after a time with an exponential distribution Exp (ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó), if the process has not jumped yet, it will jump
to state ūĚĎó.
W e use the convention that if ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 0 for some ūĚĎó ‚Č† ūĚĎĖ , this means we will never jump from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎó. If for
someūĚĎĖwe have ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 0 for al lūĚĎó ‚Č† ūĚĎĖ , then we stay in state ūĚĎĖforever, and ūĚĎĖis an absorbing state.
So from state ūĚĎĖ, there are many other states ūĚĎówe could jump to, each waiting for a time Exp (ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó). Which
of these times will be up first, leading the process to jump to that state? And how long will it be
until that first time is up and we move? The answer is given by Theorem 14.2 , which showed us the
distribution of the minimum of the Exp (ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó)‚Äôs is itself exponential, with rate
ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚ą∂= ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎó‚Č†ūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó.
F urther, and also from Theorem 14.2 , the probability that we move to some state ūĚĎó ‚Č† ūĚĎĖ is
ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó‚ą∂=ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó
‚ąĎūĚĎó‚Č†ūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó=ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó
ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ.
These last two displayed equations define the quantities ūĚĎěūĚĎĖandūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó. (IfūĚĎĖis an absorbing state ūĚĎĖ, then by
convenetion we put ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ= 1 andūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 0 forūĚĎó ‚Č† ūĚĎĖ .
So, this is the crucial idea: from state ūĚĎĖ, we wait for a holding time with distribution Exp (ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ), then move
to another state, choosing state ūĚĎówith probability ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó.
It will be convenient to write all the transition rates ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó down in a generator matrix Q defined as
follows: the off-diagonal entries are ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó, forūĚĎĖ ‚Č† ūĚĎó , and the diagonal entries are
ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ= ‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= ‚ąí‚ąĎ
ūĚĎó‚Č†ūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó.
In particular, the off-diagonal entries are positive (or 0), the diagonal entries are negative (or 0) and
each row adds up to 0.
One convenient way to keep track of the movement of a continuous time Markov jump process will be
to look at which states it jumps to, leaving the holding times for later consideration. That is, we can
consider the discrete time jump Markov chain (or just jump chain )(ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ)associated with (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) given
byūĚĎĆ0= ūĚĎč(0) and
ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ= state of ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) just after the ūĚĎõth jump.
83

This is a discrete time Markov chain that starts from the same place ūĚĎĆ0= ūĚĎč(0) as(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) does, and has
transitions given by ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó= ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó/ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ. (The jump chain cannot move from a state to itself.)
Once we know where the jump process will move, we then know what the next holding time will be:
from state ūĚĎó, the holding time will be Exp (ūĚĎěūĚĎó)

17.3 A brief note on explosion
There is one point we have to be a little careful when dealing with continuous time processes with
an infinite state space ‚Äď there is the potential of ‚Äúexplosion‚ÄĚ . Explosion is when the process can get
arbitrarily large in finite time. Think, for example, of a birth process with birth rates ūĚúÜūĚĎó= ūĚúÜūĚĎó2. The
ūĚĎõth birth is expected to occur at time
ūĚĎõ

ūĚúÜūĚĎõ
‚ąĎ
ūĚĎó=11
ūĚĎõ2.
But this has a finite limit as ūĚĎõ ‚Üí ‚ąě , meaning we expect to get infinitely many births in a finite amount
of time.
When explosion happens, it can then be rather diÔ¨Äicult to define what is going on. In that birth process,
for example, the state space is ūĚíģ = ‚Ą§+; but after some finite time, we have ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) > ūĚĎó for allūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ ‚Äď so
what state is the process in?
Although there are often technical fixes that can get around this problem (doing things like creating
an ‚Äúinfinity‚ÄĚ state, or restarting the process from ūĚĎč(0) again), we will not concern ourselves with them
here. W e shall normally deal with finite state spaces, where explosion is not a concern. When we do use
infinite state space models (such as birth processes, for example), we shall only look at examples that
explode with probability 0.

which are the equivalents to the ūĚĎõ-step transition probabilities in discrete time.

18.1 T ransitions in infinitesimal time periods
In the last section , we defined the continuous time Markov jump process with generator matrix Q in
terms of the holding times and the jump chain: from state ūĚĎĖ, we wait for a holding time exponentially
distributed with rate ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= ‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ, then jump to state ūĚĎówith probability ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó= ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó/ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ.
So what happens starting from state ūĚĎĖin a very small amount of time ūĚúŹ? The remaining time until a
move is still ūĚĎá ‚ąľ Exp(ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ), by the memoryless property . So the probability we do not move in the small
amount of time ūĚúŹis
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá > ūĚúŹ) = e‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚúŹ= 1‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ).
Here we have used the tail probability of the exponential distribution and the first two terms of the
T aylor series.
eūĚĎ•= 1+ūĚĎ•+ūĚĎú(ūĚĎ•) asūĚĎ• ‚Üí 0 .
The probability that we do move in the small amount of time ūĚúŹ, and that the move is to state ūĚĎóis
‚Ąô(ūĚĎá ‚Č§ ūĚúŹ)ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó= (ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ))ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó
ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎóūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ).
The probability we make two or more jumps is a lower order term ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) . Thus we have
‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ) = ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎĖ) = {1‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) forūĚĎĖ = ūĚĎó
ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎóūĚúŹ +ūĚĎú(ūĚúŹ) forūĚĎĖ ‚Č† ūĚĎó.
This is an equivalent definition of the Markov jump process.
18.2 T ransition semigroup and the forward and backward equations
Let us write ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎč(0) = ūĚĎĖ) for the transition probability over a time ūĚĎ°. This is the
continuous-time equivalent to the ūĚĎõ-step transition probability ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) we had before in discrete time, but
nowūĚĎ°can be any positive real value. It can be convenient to use the matrix form P(ūĚĎ°) = (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) ‚ą∂ ūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ) .
In discrete time, we were given a transition matrix P, and it was easy to find P(ūĚĎõ) = PūĚĎõas a matrix
power. In continuous time, we are given the generator matrix Q, so how can we find P(ūĚĎ°) from Q?
First, let us consider ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ† + ūĚĎ°) . T o get from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎóin timeūĚĎ† + ūĚĎ° , we could first go from ūĚĎĖto some ūĚĎėin
timeūĚĎ†, then from that ūĚĎėtoūĚĎóin timeūĚĎ°, and the intermediate state ūĚĎėcan be any state. So we have
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ†+ūĚĎ°) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎė‚ąąūĚíģūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė(ūĚĎ†)ūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°).
Y ou should recognise this as the Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov equations again. In matrix form, we can
write this as
P(ūĚĎ†+ūĚĎ°) = P(ūĚĎ†) P(ūĚĎ°).
This equation is sometimes called the ‚Äúsemigroup property‚ÄĚ, so we sometimes call the matrices (P(ūĚĎ°) ‚ą∂
ūĚĎ° ‚Č• 0) the transition semigroup .
Second, as in previous examples, we can try to get a differential equation for ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) by looking at an
infinitesimal increment ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ) .
W e can start with the Chapman‚ÄďKolmogorov equations. W e have
ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°+ūĚúŹ) = ‚ąĎ
ūĚĎėūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė(ūĚĎ°)ūĚĎĚūĚĎėūĚĎó(ūĚúŹ)
= ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)(1‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎóūĚúŹ)+‚ąĎ

= ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)+‚ąĎ

where we have treated the ūĚĎė = ūĚĎó term of the sum separately , and taken advantage of the fact that

ūĚúŹ.

ūĚĎėūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎė(ūĚĎ°)ūĚĎěūĚĎėūĚĎó.

18.3 Matrix exponential
In discrete time, when we are given the transition matrix P, we could find the ūĚĎõ-step transition probabil-
ities P(ūĚĎõ) using the matrix power PūĚĎõ. (At least when the state space was finite ‚Äď one has to be careful
with what the matrix power means if the state space is infinite.) In continuous time (and finite state
space), when we are given the generator matrix Q, it will turn out that a similar role is played by the
matrix exponential P(ūĚĎ°) = eūĚĎ°Q, which solves the forward and backward equations. In this way , the
generator matrix Q is a bit like ‚Äúthe logarithm of the transition matrix P‚ÄĚ ‚Äď although this is only a
metaphor, rather than a careful mathematical statement.
Let us be more formal about this. Y ou may remember that the usual exponential function is defined by
the T aylor series
eūĚĎ•= exp(ūĚĎ•) =‚ąě

meaning that AeA= eAA. This will be useful later.

that we have a solution to the forward and backward equations of P(ūĚĎ°) = eūĚĎ°Q, which also satisfies the

As a formal summary , we have the following.
Theorem 18.1. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a time homogeneous continuous time Markov jump process with generator
matrix Q. Let(P(ūĚĎ°)) be the transition semigroup, where P(ūĚĎ°) = (ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°)) is defined by ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) = ‚Ąô(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) =
ūĚĎó ‚ą£ ūĚĎč(0) = ūĚĎĖ) .
Then(P(ūĚĎ°)) is the minimal nonnegative solution to the forward equation
P‚Ä≤(ūĚĎ°) = P(ūĚĎ°) Q P(0) = I,
and is also the minimal nonnegative solution to the backward equation
P‚Ä≤(ūĚĎ°) = QP(ūĚĎ°) P(0) = I.
When the state space ūĚíģis finite, the forward and backward equations both have a unique solution given
by the matrix exponential P(ūĚĎ°) = eūĚĎ°Q.

19 Class structure and hitting times
‚ÄĘ Communicating classes
‚ÄĘ Hitting probabilities and expected hitting times
‚ÄĘ Recurrence and transience
When we studied discrete time Markov chains, we considered matters including communicating classes,
periodicity , hitting probabilities and expected hitting times, recurrence and transience, stationary distri-
butions, convergence to equilibrium, and the ergodic theorem. In this section and the next, we develop
this theory for continuous time Markov jump processes. Luckily , things will be simpler this time round,
as we will often be able to use exactly the same techniques as the discrete time case, often by looking at
the discrete time jump chain.
19.1 Communicating classes
In discrete time, we said that ūĚĎóis accessible from ūĚĎĖ, and wrote ūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó ifūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) > 0 for some ūĚĎõ. This
allowed us to split the state space into communicating classes. W e can do exactly the same in discrete
time.
Definition 19.1. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a Markov jump process on a state space ūĚíģwith transition semigroup
(P(ūĚĎ°)) . W e say that a state ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚíģ isaccessible from another state ūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚíģ , and write ūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó ifūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) > 0
for some ūĚĎ° ‚Č• 0 . IfūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó andūĚĎó ‚Üí ūĚĎĖ , we say that ūĚĎĖcommunicates with ūĚĎóand write ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó .
The equivalence relation ‚ÜĒ partitions ūĚíģinto equivalence classes, which we call communicating classes .
If all states are in the same class, the jump process is irreducible . A class ūĚź∂ isclosed ifūĚĎĖ ‚Üí ūĚĎó forūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚź∂
means that ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚź∂ also. IfūĚĎĖis a closed class {ūĚĎĖ} by itself, then ūĚĎĖis an absorbing state .
Recall that each state ūĚĎĖis in exactly one communicating class, and that communicating class is the set
of allūĚĎósuch that ūĚĎĖ ‚ÜĒ ūĚĎó .
IfūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) > 0 , then this means there is some sequence of jumps that can take us from ūĚĎĖtoūĚĎó. This means
that, letting R= (ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó)be the transition matrix for the jump chain (ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ), we have that ūĚĎĚūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎ°) > 0 for some
ūĚĎ°if and only if ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó(ūĚĎõ) > 0 for some ūĚĎõ. So the communicating classes for a Markov jump process are
exactly the same as the communicating classes in its discrete time jump chain.
F urther, since ūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎó> 0 if and only if ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó> 0 , we can tell what the communicating classes are directly from
the transition rate diagram.

19.2 A brief note on periodicity
In discrete time we had to worry about periodic behaviour, especially when considering long-term be-
haviour like the existence of an equilibrium distribution. However, for a continuous time jump process,
it is possible stay in a state for any positive real-number amount of time. Thus (with probability 1) we
never see periodic behaviour, and we do not have to worry about this.
91

This is one way in which continuous time processes are actually more pleasant to deal with than discrete
time processes.

Recall that the hitting probability ‚ĄéūĚĎĖūĚźī is the probability of reaching some state ūĚĎó ‚ąą ūĚźī at any point in

there. W e found these in discrete time by forming simultaneous equations by conditioning on the first
step.
F or hitting probabilities, we just care about where we jump to, and not how long we wait. Thus we are
free to consider only the jump chain (ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ)and its transition matrix R. Everything works exactly as in
discrete time.

19.4 Recurrence and transience
In discrete time, we said a state ūĚĎĖwas recurrent if the return probability ūĚĎöūĚĎĖ= 1 and transient if the
return probability ūĚĎöūĚĎĖ< 1 . W e take almost the same definition in continuous time ‚Äď the difference is
that if we never leave a state, because ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= 0 , we should call state ūĚĎĖrecurrent.
Definition 19.2. If a state ūĚĎĖ ‚ąą ūĚíģ has return probability ūĚĎöūĚĎĖ= 1 or hasūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= 0 , we say that ūĚĎĖisrecurrent .
IfūĚĎöūĚĎĖ< 1 , we say that ūĚĎĖistransient .
Suppose ūĚĎĖis recurrent. If the expected return time ūĚúáūĚĎĖ< ‚ąě is finite or ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= 0 , we say that ūĚĎĖispositive
recurrent . If the expected return time ūĚúáūĚĎĖ= ‚ąě is infinite, we say that ūĚĎĖisnull recurrent .
W e see that the return probability is totally determined by the jump chain, so whether a state is recurrent
or transient can be decided exactly as in the discrete case. In particular, in a communicating class either
all states are transient or all states are recurrent. As before, finite closed classes are recurrent, and
non-closed classes are transient.
Example 19.4. Picking up our example ( 19.1 again,{1,2} is not closed, so is transient, while the
absorbing state 3 is positive recurrent.
In the next section , we look at the long-term behaviour of continuous time Markov jump processes in
a similar way to discrete time Markov chains: stationary distributions, convergence to equilibrium, and
an ergodic theorem.

20 Long-term behaviour of Markov jump processes
‚ÄĘ Stationary distributions, which solve ūĚúčQ= 0
‚ÄĘ The limit theorem and the ergodic theorem
Our goal here is to develop the theory of the long-term behaviour of continuous time Markov jump
processes in the same way as we did for discrete time Markov chains.
In discrete time, we defined stationary distributions as solving ūĚúčP= ūĚúč . W e then had the limit
theorem , which told us about equilibrium distributions and the limit of ‚Ąô(ūĚĎčūĚĎõ= ūĚĎĖ) , and the ergodic
theorem , which told us about the long term proportion of time spent in each state. W e develop the
same results here.
20.1 Stationary distributions
W e start by defining a stationary distribution as before.
Definition 20.1. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a Markov jump process on a state space ūĚíģwith generator matrix Q

‚ąĎūĚĎĖ‚ąąūĚíģūĚúčūĚĎĖ= 1 . W e call ūĚúčastationary distribution if

This initially looks awkward: we are usually given a generator matrix Q, and then we might have to first

easier than that: we just have to solve ūĚúčQ= 0(where 0is the row vector of all zeros).
Theorem 20.1. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a Markov jump process on a state space ūĚíģwith generator matrix Q. If
ūĚúč = (ūĚúčūĚĎĖ)is a distribution with ‚ąĎūĚĎĖūĚúčūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎó= 0 for al lūĚĎó, thenūĚúčis a stationary distribution. In matrix form,
this condition is ūĚúčQ= 0.

As before, we have a result on the existence and uniqueness of the stationary distribution.
Theorem 20.2. Consider an irreducible Markov jump process on state space ūĚíģwith generator matrix
Q.
‚ÄĘ If the Markov jump process is positive recurrent, then a stationary distribution ūĚúčexists, is unique,
and is given by ūĚúčūĚĎĖ= 1/ūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚúáūĚĎĖ, whereūĚúáūĚĎĖis the expected return time to state ūĚĎĖ(unless the state space

As with the discrete time case, two conditions guarantee existence and uniqueness of the stationary
distribution: irreducibility and positive recurrence. Compared to the discrete case, there is an extra

until jumping.
As in the discrete time case, there is also the notion of reversibility and detailed balance .
Definition 20.2. Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be a Markov jump process on ūĚíģwith generator matrix ūĚĎĄ. If there is a
distribution ūĚúčonūĚíģsuch that ūĚĎĄ andūĚúčsatisfy the detailed balance conditions

then we say that (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) is reversible.
It is also the case that any ūĚúčsatisfying detailed balance for (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) is also a stationary distribution. W e
can check that the Markov jump process given in Example 20.1 is reversible.

can be at most one equilibrium distribution.

21.1 M/M/‚ąě infinite server process
In a mathematical model for a queue, individuals arrive, receive a service, and leave. In our first example,
individuals receive service as soon as they arrive ‚Äď so there is no actual ‚Äúqueueing‚ÄĚ . In our second example,
later, individuals will actually queue up and wait until the server is able to serve them.
Our first process is described as follows. Individuals arrive like a Poisson process of rate ūĚúÜ. Then they
receive a service which takes time Exp (ūĚúá) , independent of everything else, and then they leave.
This can be a useful model for many applications: for example, visitors watching a livestream on a website
could arrive at the website like a Poisson process of rate ūĚúÜand watch the livestream for a Exp (ūĚúá) amount
of time. Other models could include the number of students in the University library , the number of
insurance contracts held by an insurance broker, or the number of files being downloaded from a server.
The number of individuals (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) in the process (that is, receiving the service) at time ūĚĎ°is a Markov jump
process with arrival rates ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎĖ+1= ūĚúÜ . The rate of departures when ūĚĎĖindividuals are being serviced has the
rate and ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ,ūĚĎĖ‚ąí1= ūĚĎĖūĚúá , because, thanks to the memoryless property of the exponential distribution, the first
service time to finish is the minimum of ūĚĎĖremaining exponential service times, and this is exponential
with rate ūĚĎĖūĚúá. Since rows of a generator matrix sum to 0, we have ūĚĎěūĚĎĖ= ‚ąíūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎĖ= ūĚúÜ+ūĚĎĖūĚúá .

This process is called the M/M/‚ąě process.
‚ÄĘ The first M stands for memoryless arrivals , which means the arrivals process is a Poisson process,
which has exponential ‚Äď and therefore memoryless ‚Äď interarrival times.
‚ÄĘ The second M stands for memoryless service times , which means the service times are expo-
nentially distributed, and hence have the memoryless property .
‚ÄĘ The‚ąě stands for infinite servers , which means there is no limit on how many individuals can
be serviced at once.

21.2 M/M/1 single server queue
W e now consider a slightly different process with only one server. This is called an M/M/1 queue,
where the ‚Äú1‚ÄĚ is the number of servers.
As before, individuals arrive as a Poisson process of rate ūĚúÜ. If there is no one currently being serviced,
the individual goes straight to the server, and begins an Exp (ūĚúá) service time. Otherwise, they have to
join a queue waiting for the server to become free. When the server finishes serving one individual, then,
provided the queue is nonempty , they immediately begin to service the individual who has been waiting
in the queue for the longest time.
This is again a very useful model for many applications. F or example, it could model a lecturer‚Äôs oÔ¨Äice
hours: students arrive at my oÔ¨Äice at rate ūĚúÜ; if I am free, I can immediately answer their question, which
will take an Exp (ūĚúá) amount of time; but if I am already dealing with a student, they must join a queue
and wait until I am free. This could also model purchases at a shop with a single till, phone calls to a
receptionist, or the work of a self-employed person on different tasks.
Let(ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) be the number of individuals in the process at time ūĚĎ°. That could be ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 0 , if the server
is free;ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = 1 if one individual is currently being serviced but no one else is waiting; or ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°) = ūĚĎ• > 1

The first thing we would like to know is if the queue keeps getting longer and longer, or whether eventually
every individual is serviced.
F or this continuous time jump process (ūĚĎč(ūĚĎ°)) , the discrete time jump chain (ūĚĎĆūĚĎõ)is a simple random walk
with up probability ūĚĎĚ = ūĚúÜ/(ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá) , down probability ūĚĎě = ūĚúá/(ūĚúÜ+ūĚúá) , and a reflecting barrier at 0. Recall
that this Markov chain is transient for ūĚĎĚ > ūĚĎě , null recurrent for ūĚĎĚ = ūĚĎě , and positive recurrent for ūĚĎĚ < ūĚĎě .
Thus we see that:
‚ÄĘ F orūĚúÜ > ūĚúá , the queue is transient, and eventually the length of the queue will grow longer and
longer and never clear. This is clearly highly undesirable.
‚ÄĘ F orūĚúÜ = ūĚúá , the queue is null recurrent, so while the queue will eventually clear, the expected time
for it to do so is infinite, so the queue will get very long, and individuals will may have to wait for
an extremely long time. This is also undesirable.
‚ÄĘ F orūĚúÜ < ūĚúá , the queue is positive recurrent, so while individuals may sometimes have to wait, the
queue will clear fairly regularly . This is what we want.
In what follows, we consider only the ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ case ūĚúÜ < ūĚúá . Again we have two questions:
1. In the long run, what is the average number of individuals being serviced?
2. In the long run, for what proportion of the time is the server idle and for what proportion of time
is the server busy?
Again, we look for a stationary distribution, which will tell us about the long term behaviour of the

(d) How might you improve the model of a shop with two cash registers to more accurately reflect true
queueing behaviour? What effect would this have on the average wait time?
Solution. W e might assume that customers join the shortest queue, rather than picking one at random.
W e might assume a customer will move to the other till if it becomes empty . W e might even assume that
customers will change queues if the other queue gets shorter than the one they are currently in. In all
cases, the average waiting time will go down.
